text:
Lemaire Exports Inc. sues Kwon Shipping Ltd. on three causes of action: breach of contract, conversion, and unjust enrichment. On a summary judgment motion, the motion judge finds that the breach of contract claim is clearly established on the record with no genuine issue requiring a trial, but that the conversion and unjust enrichment claims raise genuine factual disputes that require trial. Lemaire asks the court to grant summary judgment on the breach of contract claim alone.

May the court grant summary judgment on only the breach of contract claim while directing a trial on the remaining claims?
opts: No — summary judgment must dispose of the entire action; a court cannot grant it on only some claims.

Yes — r. 20.04(3) of the Rules of Civil Procedure expressly authorises the court to grant summary judgment on part of a claim or some of the claims in the action, and to direct a trial on the remaining issues.
Yes, but only if Kwon Shipping consents to having the breach of contract claim resolved by summary judgment.
No — granting summary judgment on one claim while the other claims proceed to trial would be procedurally unfair to the defendant.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(3), the court may grant summary judgment in respect of some claims or some issues while directing a trial on the remaining claims or issues. This partial summary judgment power enables courts to resolve discrete issues that are clearly established without requiring the entire action to proceed to trial. Courts retain a discretion to decline partial summary judgment where granting it would fragment the proceedings and increase overall cost or delay, but where a claim is cleanly separable, partial summary judgment is expressly authorised.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.04(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Partial Summary Judgment

text:
Priya was sexually assaulted by her employer in 2015. She did not report the assault at the time. In 2024, following therapy, she comes to understand the full psychological harm caused to her and wishes to bring a civil claim.

Which statement best describes the applicable limitation period for Priya's claim?
opts: The claim is statute-barred because more than two years have passed since the assault occurred in 2015.
No limitation period applies to Priya's claim because sexual assault falls within s. 16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002.
The discoverability principle defers the start of the two-year period to 2024, so Priya has until 2026 to commence her action.
A 15-year ultimate limitation period applies to Priya's claim, which will expire in 2030.
expl: Section 16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002 provides that there is no limitation period with respect to a claim based on a sexual assault. This removes the limitation period entirely — not merely defers it via discoverability. Furthermore, s. 16(1)(h) also excludes such claims from the 15-year ultimate limitation period in s. 15 of the Act. Priya may commence her claim at any time.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16(1)(h)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Claims with No Limitation Period

text:
On a motion for an interlocutory injunction, the defendant argues that the plaintiff must demonstrate it is more likely than not to succeed at trial in order to satisfy the first branch of the RJR-MacDonald test.

Which statement correctly describes the 'serious question to be tried' branch of the RJR-MacDonald test?
opts: The plaintiff must show it is more likely than not to succeed at the trial of the action.
The plaintiff must show its claim is not frivolous or vexatious; this is a deliberately low threshold that does not require the court to assess the merits in any depth.
The plaintiff must establish a strong prima facie case, demonstrating that it would likely obtain a permanent injunction at trial.
The plaintiff must produce evidence that would be sufficient to convince a jury of the facts underlying the claim.
expl: The Supreme Court of Canada in RJR-MacDonald confirmed that the 'serious question to be tried' is a low threshold. The moving party need only show that the claim is neither frivolous nor vexatious. The court is not to engage in an extensive review of the merits at this stage. A probability of success is not required. The strong prima facie case standard is reserved for mandatory injunctions and certain exceptional categories.
caseRef: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Interlocutory Injunctions — RJR-MacDonald Test

text:
Sylviane operates a catering business. Her supplier breached a contract on June 1, 2023, and she discovered the breach on the same day. She did not commence an action. In January 2025, her accountant told her she should sue to recover her losses. Sylviane commences a breach of contract action on June 15, 2025.

Which statement is correct regarding Sylviane's action?
opts: The action is not statute-barred because the accountant's advice in January 2025 restarted the limitation period.
The action is statute-barred because more than two years passed since the claim was discovered on June 1, 2023.
A six-year limitation period applies to breach of contract claims in Ontario.
The limitation period was suspended while Sylviane sought professional advice about whether to sue.
expl: Sylviane discovered her breach of contract claim on June 1, 2023. Under s. 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002, she had two years — until June 1, 2025 — to commence proceedings. Commencing on June 15, 2025 is 14 days after the expiry of the limitation period. The action is therefore statute-barred. An accountant's advice does not restart or suspend the limitation period.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period

text:
During examination for discovery of the plaintiff, Adeyemi Real Estate Corp., counsel for the defendant Borowski Development Ltd. asks a question and Adeyemi's counsel instructs the witness not to answer on the ground of relevance. The refusal is recorded on the refusals chart. At the conclusion of the examination, Borowski's counsel brings a motion to compel the answer. The motion judge upholds Adeyemi's refusal, finding the question is not relevant to any matter in issue.

What is the procedural consequence when a court upholds a refusal on a motion to compel answers under r. 31.08?
opts: The examining party is entitled to re-attend discovery and ask the same question again in a differently worded form.
The court must adjourn the action pending a further review of whether all refusals were properly upheld.
Where the court upholds the refusal, the examining party is not entitled to re-attend discovery to ask the refused question, and the refused question is resolved without further re-attendance.
The court is required to award costs to the refusing party whenever a refusal is upheld on a motion to compel.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.08, on a motion to compel answers to questions refused at discovery, the court may order the question to be answered or may uphold the refusal. Where the court upholds the refusal — finding the question was properly refused, for example on grounds of irrelevance — the examining party is not entitled to re-attend discovery to pose the same question or effectively the same question in different words. The upheld refusal is a final determination on that question, and no further re-attendance on that issue is required.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 31.08
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Refusals and Undertakings at Discovery

text:
Tanaka Leasing Corp. sues Bruneau Auto Group Ltd. on a claim involving a disputed equipment lease. Bruneau cross-claims against its co-defendant Patel Logistics Inc. for contribution. Tanaka moves for partial summary judgment solely against Bruneau on the issue of Bruneau's liability under the lease, while agreeing that damages and the cross-claim between Bruneau and Patel will proceed to trial. Bruneau's counsel advises the client that partial summary judgment is inappropriate in Ontario because the rule requires summary judgment to resolve the entire action.

Is Bruneau's counsel correct that summary judgment must always dispose of the entire action?
opts: Yes — Ontario courts have consistently held that summary judgment must resolve every claim and every party or it cannot be granted.
Yes — where there is a cross-claim outstanding between co-defendants, no summary judgment of any kind may be granted until the cross-claim is also resolved.

No — r. 20.04(3) of the Rules of Civil Procedure expressly authorises the court to grant summary judgment on some claims or against some parties, directing a trial on the remainder; partial summary judgment on Bruneau's liability is legally available, subject to the court's discretion.
No — but the court may only grant partial summary judgment if Tanaka discontinues its claim against Patel Logistics before the motion is heard.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(3), summary judgment may be granted on a claim, a part of a claim, or against some but not all parties, while directing a trial on the remaining matters. Counsel's advice that summary judgment must always dispose of the entire action is incorrect and contradicts the express text of r. 20.04(3). Courts will exercise their discretion to grant partial summary judgment where doing so is just and does not unnecessarily fragment the proceedings or increase cost. The existence of a cross-claim between co-defendants does not automatically bar partial summary judgment against one defendant on a discrete issue.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.04(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Partial Summary Judgment

text:
Marco fell from scaffolding at a construction site on May 1, 2022 and was hospitalized with serious injuries. He knew immediately that the contractor had erected the scaffolding and that the fall had caused his injuries. He was not aware until January 10, 2024 that the contractor had violated OSHA regulations.

On what date was Marco's claim against the contractor 'discovered' for the purposes of the Limitations Act, 2002?
opts: January 10, 2024
The date his lawyer first advised him to sue
The date an OSHA investigation report was issued
expl: Under s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim is discovered on the day the person first knew or ought to have known: (a) the injury occurred; (b) the injury was caused by the act or omission of the defendant; (c) the identity of the defendant; and (d) that a proceeding would be an appropriate means to remedy the harm. Marco knew all four elements on May 1, 2022: he was injured, the contractor caused his injury, he knew the contractor's identity, and a proceeding was appropriate. Knowledge of a specific OSHA violation is not a required element of discovery.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Discoverability — Section 5

text:
A defendant in a civil action argues that the plaintiff's claim is statute-barred because the alleged act or omission occurred more than two years ago. The plaintiff argues that she did not discover her claim until more recently.

Who bears the burden of proving that the claim was discovered on a date later than the date the act or omission occurred?
opts: The defendant must prove the claim was discovered on or before the date of the act or omission
Neither party; the court determines the date of discovery independently based on the evidence
The plaintiff, who must rebut the presumption in s. 5(2) of the Limitations Act, 2002 that the claim was discovered on the day the act or omission occurred
The burden shifts between the parties depending on which party has better access to the relevant evidence
expl: Section 5(2) of the Limitations Act, 2002 creates a presumption that a claim is presumed to have been discovered on the day the act or omission occurred. A plaintiff who wishes to rely on a later discovery date must rebut this presumption. The burden therefore rests on the plaintiff to establish that she did not know and could not reasonably have known the relevant facts on the earlier date.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Discoverability — Section 5

text:
The plaintiff successfully moves for summary judgment on the issue of liability. However, the parties dispute the quantum of damages, and the motion judge finds there is a genuine issue requiring a trial on that question.

Can the court grant summary judgment on the issue of liability alone and direct that the issue of damages proceed to trial?
opts: No — summary judgment must resolve all issues in the action or it cannot be granted at all.
Yes — r. 20.04(3) of the Rules of Civil Procedure permits the court to grant judgment on some issues while directing a trial on the remaining issues.
No — granting judgment on liability alone would deprive the defendant of the right to a full trial on all issues in the action.
Only if both parties consent to bifurcating liability and damages.
expl: Rule 20.04(3) of the Rules of Civil Procedure expressly permits partial summary judgment. The court may grant judgment in respect of some claims or some parties and direct a trial for the remaining issues. This allows courts to resolve discrete issues efficiently while preserving the right to a trial on genuinely contested questions. Partial summary judgment on liability, with damages to be determined at trial, is a recognized and permitted procedure.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | No Genuine Issue Requiring a Trial

text:
Giordano Wealth Management Corp. successfully defended a securities misrepresentation lawsuit brought by Tremblay Investment Fund LP three years ago. The action was dismissed after a full trial, and the matter has been concluded. Giordano now faces a new claim by a different plaintiff, Nakamura Pension Trust, arising from the same alleged misrepresentation. Nakamura seeks production of legal memoranda that Giordano's outside counsel prepared in connection with the now-concluded Tremblay litigation, arguing that solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege both expired when the Tremblay action ended.

Is Nakamura's argument correct?
opts: Yes — both solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege expire when the litigation to which they relate concludes; all documents from the Tremblay action must now be produced.
No — both solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege are permanent and survive the conclusion of all related proceedings.

Partly — litigation privilege over the memoranda expired when the Tremblay action concluded, but solicitor-client privilege remains intact and continues to protect those memoranda in the new Nakamura proceeding.
Partly — solicitor-client privilege expired with the Tremblay action because it was created in a litigation context, but litigation privilege survives and continues to protect the memoranda.
expl: Under Ontario privilege law, solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege are distinct doctrines with different durations. Solicitor-client privilege is permanent — it survives the conclusion of the retainer, the litigation, and the client relationship. It cannot be eroded by the passage of time or the end of proceedings. Litigation privilege, by contrast, is temporary — as confirmed in Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39, it exists to protect the adversarial process and expires when the litigation for which the documents were created has concluded. Accordingly, litigation privilege over the Tremblay memoranda ended when that action was dismissed, but solicitor-client privilege over any portions that constitute confidential legal advice communications remains fully intact and protects those documents from production in the Nakamura proceeding.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2); Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery

text:
Delorme Logistics Inc. is a defendant in a commercial fraud action. During preparation of the affidavit of documents, counsel identifies two categories of documents: (A) emails between Delorme's CEO and outside counsel discussing the CEO's litigation strategy and legal exposure — created after the claim was issued; and (B) a legal memorandum prepared by outside counsel advising Delorme on the legality of its pricing practices, written eight months before any litigation was contemplated.

Which statement correctly describes the privilege status of each category of documents?
opts: Category A is protected by litigation privilege only; Category B is not protected by any privilege because it predates the litigation.

Category A is protected by both solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege; Category B is protected by solicitor-client privilege only. Both must be listed in Schedule B but need not be produced.
Category A is protected by litigation privilege only, which will expire when the litigation ends; Category B is not privileged at all.
Neither category is privileged because Delorme is a corporation and the privilege belongs to the individual officers, not the entity.
expl: Under settled Ontario privilege law, Category A — litigation strategy communications between the CEO and outside counsel after the claim was issued — satisfies both solicitor-client privilege (confidential lawyer-client communications for legal advice) and litigation privilege (documents prepared for the dominant purpose of litigation, per Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39). Category B — the pre-litigation legal memorandum on pricing legality — satisfies solicitor-client privilege because it is a confidential communication between lawyer and client for the purpose of obtaining legal advice; this privilege is permanent and applies regardless of whether litigation was anticipated. Litigation privilege does not apply to Category B because litigation was not anticipated when it was created. Both documents must be listed in Schedule B of the affidavit of documents under r. 30.03(2).
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2); Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery

text:
Barrani Construction Inc. moves for summary judgment to dismiss a claim by Okafor Building Supplies Ltd. for unpaid invoices. Barrani files a detailed affidavit from its president denying that any contract was formed. Okafor's counsel serves a responding record that consists only of the statement of claim and the statement of defence, with no affidavit or other evidence.

What is the consequence of Okafor relying solely on its pleadings as its responding record on the summary judgment motion?
opts: Relying on pleadings is sufficient; pleadings are sworn documents that constitute evidence on a motion.

Okafor's approach is improper — under r. 20.02(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, a responding party cannot rest on its pleadings but must set out specific facts by affidavit or other evidence showing a genuine issue requiring a trial.
The motion must be dismissed because the moving party bears the sole burden of establishing there is no genuine issue requiring a trial.
The motion judge must adjourn the motion to allow Okafor additional time to file evidence before ruling.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.02(2), a party responding to a summary judgment motion cannot simply rely on its pleadings. The responding party must set out in its evidence by affidavit, transcript of cross-examination, or other means the specific facts showing that there is a genuine issue requiring a trial. This is the 'best foot forward' obligation recognised in Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7. Pleadings are not evidence; relying on them alone leaves the court with no basis to find a genuine issue on the responding side.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.02(2); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Evidence on Summary Judgment Motion

text:
Kwiatkowski Property Inc. is examined for discovery in a construction lien action. During the examination, Kwiatkowski's representative gives an undertaking to provide the original cost estimates within 30 days. Three months later, the estimates have not been delivered. Opposing counsel for Nguyen Builders Ltd. wants to compel compliance.

What procedural step can Nguyen Builders take to enforce the outstanding undertaking?
opts: Nguyen Builders may simply re-attend at examination for discovery and ask for the documents again; no motion is required.
Nguyen Builders must commence a new action against Kwiatkowski for breach of the undertaking.
Nguyen Builders may bring a motion under r. 31.08 of the Rules of Civil Procedure to compel Kwiatkowski to answer the undertaking, and the court may order compliance along with costs.
Nguyen Builders must wait until the pre-trial conference and raise the failure to fulfil the undertaking with the pre-trial judge.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.08, where a party refuses to answer a question or fails to comply with an obligation arising from the examination for discovery — including failing to fulfil an undertaking — the examining party may bring a motion to compel compliance. The court may order that the question be answered or the undertaking be fulfilled, and may award costs of the motion against the defaulting party. An outstanding undertaking that is not fulfilled within the agreed or reasonable time can be the subject of such a motion without the need to re-attend discovery.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 31.08
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Refusals and Undertakings at Discovery

text:
Nakagawa Developments Ltd. moves for summary judgment against Petrov Contracting Inc. on a claim for breach of a construction contract. Nakagawa files an affidavit from its project manager setting out the contractual obligations and the alleged breaches. Petrov responds with an affidavit from its site supervisor stating that all work was completed to specification but providing no documentary records or photographs to support this assertion.

Which statement best describes the obligation of both parties with respect to evidence on a summary judgment motion under r. 20.02?
opts: Only the moving party (Nakagawa) has an obligation to file evidence; the responding party need not adduce any evidence until trial.
Both parties must put their best foot forward with all evidence available to them; neither may rely on the mere assertion that evidence exists without placing it before the court.

Petrov's affidavit from its site supervisor is sufficient to defeat the motion regardless of the absence of documentary support, because oral evidence from a percipient witness always creates a genuine issue.
The motion judge must accept Petrov's affidavit at face value without assessing whether the sworn statements are internally consistent or credible.
expl: Under r. 20.02(1) and r. 20.02(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, both the moving and responding parties are required to put their best foot forward and present all evidence available to them. The Supreme Court of Canada confirmed this mutual obligation in Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7. Neither party may keep evidence in reserve for trial. A motion judge is entitled to assess whether the responding party's evidence is sufficient to show a genuine issue, and a bare assertion without supporting documentation may be given limited weight.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.02(1), r. 20.02(2); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Evidence on Summary Judgment Motion

text:
A defendant moves for summary judgment, arguing that the plaintiff's claim has no merit and should be dismissed without trial. The plaintiff has not filed any responding evidence.

What is the legal test a motion judge applies on a summary judgment motion under the Rules of Civil Procedure?
opts: Whether the plaintiff's claim has less than a 50% probability of success at trial.
Whether there is a genuine issue requiring a trial, assessed by asking whether the court can make a fair and just determination on the record under the Hryniak v Mauldin framework.
Whether the defendant can prove its defence on a balance of probabilities.
Whether the plaintiff can establish a prima facie case for its claim at the motion stage.
expl: Rule 20.04(2)(a) of the Rules of Civil Procedure provides that the court shall grant summary judgment if it is satisfied there is no genuine issue requiring a trial. In Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that the governing question is whether the motion judge can achieve a fair and just adjudication on the motion record — a culture shift toward proportionate, accessible, and timely justice.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(2)(a); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | No Genuine Issue Requiring a Trial

text:
Srivastava Realty Corp. moves for summary judgment on its claim for specific performance of an agreement of purchase and sale. Srivastava files a thorough affidavit with supporting documents. The defendant, Delacroix Estate Inc., files a responding affidavit but leaves out several internal emails that would support the defence because counsel decides to 'keep them for trial.' The motion is heard, and the motion judge grants summary judgment against Delacroix.

What does the 'best foot forward' obligation mean in the context of a responding party on a summary judgment motion, and what consequence follows if the responding party withholds available evidence?
opts: The best foot forward obligation means the responding party must file at least one affidavit; it may save other evidence for trial without consequence.

The best foot forward obligation requires the responding party to present all evidence available to it; if it withholds evidence and summary judgment is granted, the party cannot rely on that withheld evidence to seek a new trial.
The best foot forward obligation applies only to expert evidence; lay affidavit evidence may be withheld for trial at the party's discretion.
The best foot forward obligation requires only that the responding party disclose the existence of withheld evidence so the court can decide whether to order its production.
expl: Under r. 20.02(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, and as confirmed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7, the 'best foot forward' obligation requires each party to put before the court all evidence available to it that is relevant to the motion. The responding party cannot strategically withhold evidence hoping to use it at trial. If summary judgment is granted because the responding party failed to present its full case, that party cannot subsequently seek to set aside the judgment on the basis of the evidence it deliberately withheld — it is bound by its failure to comply with the obligation.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.02(2); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Evidence on Summary Judgment Motion

text:
Priya seeks an interlocutory injunction to prevent a competitor from using her confidential client list pending the outcome of the trial. The motion is heard by a judge in the Ontario Superior Court of Justice.

What three-part test must Priya satisfy to obtain the interlocutory injunction?
opts: She must prove on a balance of probabilities that she will succeed at trial.

She must satisfy the three-part test from RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG): (1) serious question to be tried, (2) irreparable harm if the injunction is refused, and (3) the balance of convenience favours granting the injunction.
She must prove her right to the relief claimed beyond a reasonable doubt.
She must establish a prima facie case for the relief claimed and demonstrate a preponderance of evidence in her favour.
expl: The governing test for an interlocutory injunction in Ontario is the three-part test established by the Supreme Court of Canada in RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311: (1) Is there a serious question to be tried? (2) Will the moving party suffer irreparable harm if the injunction is refused? (3) Does the balance of convenience favour granting or refusing the injunction?
caseRef: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Interlocutory Injunctions — RJR-MacDonald Test

text:
Reuben is owed $10,000 under a contract made in 2000. He never commenced an action to recover the debt. It is now 2026. Reuben argues that the Limitations Act, 2002 does not apply to his claim because the contract was made before the Act came into force on January 1, 2004.

Which is the most accurate statement regarding Reuben's claim?
opts: The former Limitations Act (with its six-year limitation period) applies to contracts made before January 1, 2004, so Reuben may still have time to sue.
The Limitations Act, 2002 does not apply retroactively to claims that arose before January 1, 2004.
Reuben's claim is statute-barred; the Limitations Act, 2002 and its transitional rules extinguished pre-existing claims where the applicable limitation period had already expired.
Reuben can rely on an acknowledgment of the debt made by the debtor at any time to restart the limitation period, even now.
expl: The Limitations Act, 2002 contains transitional provisions that address pre-existing claims. Even applying the former six-year limitation period to a 2000 contract claim, that period would have expired by 2006 at the latest. By 2026, Reuben's claim is long extinguished. Moreover, once a limitation period has expired and a claim is statute-barred, a subsequent acknowledgment of the debt cannot revive it — acknowledgment must occur within the running limitation period to restart the clock.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4; transitional provisions
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period

text:
A defendant is served with a summary judgment motion. The defendant receives proper notice of the motion but fails to file any responding evidence. The plaintiff's evidence is detailed and uncontradicted.

What should the court do in these circumstances?
opts: Adjourn the motion to give the defendant a further opportunity to respond.
Dismiss the motion because summary judgment requires the consent of both parties.
Deny summary judgment automatically because every defendant has an absolute right to a trial.
Grant summary judgment if the plaintiff's uncontradicted evidence establishes there is no genuine issue requiring a trial.
expl: Where a defendant has been properly served with a summary judgment motion and fails to file responding evidence, the court may proceed on the motion. If the plaintiff's uncontradicted evidence demonstrates there is no genuine issue requiring a trial, the court may grant summary judgment under r. 20.04(2)(a). The defendant's failure to put its best foot forward does not automatically result in an adjournment or dismissal of the motion.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(2)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | No Genuine Issue Requiring a Trial

text:
During examination for discovery, counsel for Riverbend Contracting Ltd. refuses to allow the company's representative to answer a question on the basis that the answer might be harmful to Riverbend's case. Opposing counsel asks what reason Riverbend is providing for the refusal so it can be tracked on the refusals chart.

When a party refuses to answer a question at examination for discovery, what must that party do under the Rules of Civil Procedure?
opts: The refusing party need not provide any reason; a simple refusal is sufficient and the examining party must bring a motion if it wants the question answered.
The refusing party must state the reason for the refusal; under r. 31.07 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, if a question is refused, the reason for the refusal must be given.
The refusing party must provide a sworn affidavit at the examination explaining why the question is being refused.
The refusing party must obtain a court order permitting the refusal before refusing to answer at the examination.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.07, where a person being examined for discovery refuses to answer a question, the reason for the refusal must be stated. Common grounds include privilege, irrelevance, and oppressiveness. This obligation to state the reason enables the examining party to assess whether to challenge the refusal and allows the refusal to be properly tracked on the refusals chart. A bare refusal without a stated reason is not compliant with r. 31.07.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 31.07
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Refusals and Undertakings at Discovery

text:
Examination for discovery of the defendant, Paradis Fabrication Ltd., was completed fourteen months ago in a commercial negligence action brought by Sobchak Industrial Supply Inc. At the time of discovery, Sobchak's counsel noted seventeen questions that were refused on grounds of relevance and recorded them on the refusals chart. Sobchak's counsel has not taken any steps to challenge any of the refusals since the examination. Trial is now scheduled for three months away, and Sobchak's counsel brings a motion to compel answers to all seventeen refused questions.

What is the risk Sobchak faces in bringing a motion to compel answers to refused questions fourteen months after the examination for discovery?
opts: There is no time limit on bringing a motion to compel answers to refused questions; Sobchak may bring the motion at any time before trial.
Sobchak's motion will automatically be dismissed because a motion to compel answers to refused questions must be brought within 60 days of the discovery.

Sobchak risks the motion being dismissed or penalized in costs on the basis that the motion was not brought within a reasonable time after the examination for discovery, causing prejudice to Paradis and disrupting the trial preparation schedule.
Sobchak must obtain leave of the court to compel answers to any discovery refusals if more than six months have passed since the examination.
expl: Under Ontario discovery practice, while there is no hard statutory deadline for bringing a motion to compel answers to discovery refusals, courts expect that such motions be brought within a reasonable time after the examination. Waiting fourteen months — until three months before trial — creates a real risk that the court will find the delay unreasonable, that prejudice has resulted to the responding party, and that granting the motion would disrupt the trial schedule. Courts have dismissed or imposed adverse costs consequences on late-brought motions to compel. Sobchak's delay in challenging the refusals for over a year is a significant procedural risk, and the court may decline to hear the motion or impose costs regardless of the merits of the individual refusals.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 31.07; r. 31.08
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Refusals and Undertakings at Discovery

text:
Amara seeks an ex parte interlocutory injunction — without prior notice to the other side — to freeze her former business partner's bank account. She believes the partner is about to transfer assets out of the jurisdiction.

What additional considerations apply when a moving party seeks an ex parte interlocutory injunction?
opts: Ex parte injunctions are not available in commercial disputes; Amara must give notice to the other side.
On an ex parte motion, the moving party need only satisfy the regular RJR-MacDonald test because the other side can bring a motion to set aside the order if it disagrees.

On an ex parte motion, the moving party must demonstrate urgency making prior notice impracticable and must make full and frank disclosure of all material facts, including facts that may favour the other side.
Ex parte injunctions are permanent orders unless the opposing party obtains a court order to vary or set them aside.
expl: Ex parte injunctions are exceptional because they are granted without hearing from the opposing party. Two additional duties attach beyond the standard RJR-MacDonald test. First, the moving party must demonstrate urgency sufficient to justify dispensing with notice — typically that giving notice would cause irreparable harm (e.g., dissipation of assets). Second, and critically, the moving party owes a duty of full and frank disclosure to the court: it must disclose all material facts, including those that favour the opposing party, because the court cannot hear the other side. Breach of this duty can result in the injunction being set aside.
caseRef: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311; Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 37.07
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Interlocutory Injunctions — RJR-MacDonald Test

text:
Abramowitz Financial Services Ltd. brings a summary judgment motion against Guerriero Holdings Inc. on a claim for unjust enrichment. The motion record contains competing affidavits from the key witnesses on each side, and the motion judge is satisfied that the key issue turns entirely on the credibility of the two affiants — specifically, which party's version of a disputed oral agreement is more reliable. The motion judge is inclined to resolve the credibility issue on the record without hearing live testimony.

What power does the motion judge have to hear oral evidence in order to resolve this credibility issue on the summary judgment motion?
opts: The motion judge has no power to order oral evidence on a summary judgment motion; live testimony is reserved for trial.
The motion judge may order oral evidence at any time on a summary judgment motion without any precondition.

Under r. 20.04(2.2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, the motion judge may order that oral evidence be heard to determine whether there is a genuine issue requiring a trial, subject to any directions the judge considers appropriate.
The motion judge may only receive oral evidence if both parties have agreed in writing to conduct the summary judgment motion as a mini-trial.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(2.2), where the motion judge exercises the expanded powers under r. 20.04(2.1) and determines that oral evidence is required to determine whether there is a genuine issue requiring a trial, the judge may order that oral evidence be presented, subject to any directions the judge considers appropriate. This provision gives the motion judge a discretionary power to receive viva voce evidence where credibility cannot fairly be resolved on the paper record alone. The power is discretionary and is exercised when necessary to achieve a fair determination.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.04(2.2)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Evidence on Summary Judgment Motion

text:
During examination for discovery, the plaintiff's counsel asks the defendant about a memorandum prepared by the defendant's solicitor in connection with the litigation. The plaintiff acknowledges the memorandum is likely subject to solicitor-client privilege but asks whether the memorandum exists and what its general subject matter is.

Must the defendant disclose the existence and general subject matter of the privileged memorandum?
opts: No — solicitor-client privilege protects both the existence and the content of a privileged document from disclosure.
No — the plaintiff cannot ask any questions that touch on a potentially privileged document.

Yes — under r. 31.06(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, a party must answer questions about the existence, content, and location of relevant documents even if those documents are privileged; privilege protects the content from disclosure, not the existence.
Only if the court has first ordered production of the document after reviewing it in camera.
expl: Rule 31.06(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure expressly provides that a person may be questioned about the existence and location of documents that are relevant to a matter in issue, even if those documents are privileged. Solicitor-client privilege protects the content of the communication from disclosure; it does not shield the mere existence of the document from being acknowledged. The defendant must confirm the memorandum exists and identify its general subject matter, but may claim privilege over its contents.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.06(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Examination for Discovery | Scope — Relevance to Any Matter in Issue

text:
In a contract dispute, the plaintiff asks the defendant during examination for discovery about conversations the defendant had with third-party suppliers that may reveal the basis for the contract price. The defendant refuses to answer, arguing that these conversations are irrelevant because they are not specifically pleaded in either party's pleadings.

How should the defendant's refusal to answer be assessed?
opts: The refusal is proper because parties are only required to answer questions about facts specifically pleaded in their pleadings.
The refusal is proper if the conversations are not listed in the defendant's affidavit of documents.
If the conversations are relevant to any issue in the action — such as the meaning of the contract or the quantum of damages — the refusal is improper under r. 31.06(1).
The refusal is proper unless the defendant previously identified the conversations in a will-say statement filed before trial.
expl: The scope of examination for discovery under r. 31.06(1) is not limited to pleaded facts. If the defendant's conversations with third-party suppliers are relevant to an issue in the action — such as the proper interpretation of the contract or the quantum of the plaintiff's damages — the defendant is obliged to answer questions about them. The refusal to answer is improper if relevance to a matter in issue can be established.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.06(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Examination for Discovery | Scope — Relevance to Any Matter in Issue

text:
On a summary judgment motion, the defendant argues that because the affiants on each side give conflicting accounts of the same events, a credibility dispute exists that makes a trial mandatory. The defendant relies on pre-Hryniak case law for this proposition.

Is the defendant's argument correct after Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7?
opts: Yes — credibility disputes between affiants always require a trial in Ontario.

No — after Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7, a credibility dispute does not automatically preclude summary judgment; the motion judge may assess credibility under r. 20.04(2.1) if it would not be unjust to do so.
Yes — the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the right to present live testimony before any adverse credibility finding is made.
No — but the motion judge may assess credibility only if the opposing party has waived its right to cross-examine the affiants.
expl: Before Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7, it was widely accepted in Ontario that credibility disputes automatically necessitated a trial. Hryniak changed this. The Supreme Court held that motion judges, exercising the expanded powers under r. 20.04(2.1), may assess credibility on a summary judgment motion if doing so would not be unjust. A credibility dispute is therefore not an automatic bar to summary judgment; the motion judge must assess whether resolving the credibility issue without a trial would be fair in the circumstances.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(2.1); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | No Genuine Issue Requiring a Trial

text:
During examination for discovery in a personal injury action, the plaintiff's lawyer asks the defendant driver about a prior driving incident that occurred two years before the accident in question. Defense counsel objects on the ground that the prior incident was not pleaded in either party's pleadings.

Which statement best describes the proper scope of questions at an examination for discovery under the Rules of Civil Procedure?
opts: Questions must be limited to the specific facts pleaded in the statement of claim and statement of defence.
A party may be examined on any matter relevant to any matter in issue in the action, which may extend beyond the specific facts pleaded in the pleadings.
Questions may cover any matter the examining party considers helpful to its case, with no relevance requirement.
Examination for discovery is limited to facts the opposing party indicated in its will-say statements that it intends to rely on at trial.
expl: Rule 31.06(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure provides that a party examined for discovery shall answer questions concerning any matter that is relevant to any matter in issue between or among the parties in the action. The scope is broader than the specific facts pleaded — it extends to any matter relevant to an issue in the action. However, relevance to an issue in the action remains the touchstone; pure fishing expeditions are not permitted.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.06(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Examination for Discovery | Scope — Relevance to Any Matter in Issue

text:
Kwame seeks an interlocutory injunction to prevent a buyer from taking possession of his business, which he alleges was sold under duress. The defendant argues that any loss Kwame suffers before trial can be fully compensated by a damages award.

How does the 'irreparable harm' requirement of the RJR-MacDonald test apply in this situation?
opts: Irreparable harm is satisfied by showing that any harm will occur before trial, regardless of whether damages would be adequate.
The court should grant the injunction to preserve the status quo regardless of whether a damages award would adequately compensate Kwame.
Irreparable harm exists only where the physical harm to the moving party is permanent and cannot be undone.
Kwame must show that the harm he will suffer cannot be adequately compensated by an award of damages at trial; if damages would be adequate, he does not satisfy this branch of the test.
expl: The 'irreparable harm' branch of the RJR-MacDonald test requires the moving party to demonstrate that, if the injunction is refused, the harm suffered pending trial cannot be adequately remedied by damages. If a damages award at trial would fully compensate the moving party, the harm is not 'irreparable' and this branch is not satisfied. The court is not required to preserve the status quo in all cases; the adequacy of damages is the central question.
caseRef: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Interlocutory Injunctions — RJR-MacDonald Test

text:
Volkov Property Management Inc. sues Achebe Construction Group Ltd. for $2.1 million arising from a large commercial renovation project. There are six issues in dispute. Volkov moves for partial summary judgment on one narrow issue — whether a specific variation order was validly executed — while acknowledging that the remaining five issues require trial. The motion judge must decide whether to exercise the discretion to grant partial summary judgment on the variation order issue.

What is the key consideration a court applies when exercising its discretion to grant or refuse partial summary judgment under r. 20.04(3)?
opts: The court must grant partial summary judgment whenever the moving party establishes its position on any single issue; the motion judge has no discretion to refuse.
The court considers only whether the moving party can establish the issue on a balance of probabilities at the summary judgment stage.

The court considers whether granting partial summary judgment would fragment the proceedings, create additional delay or expense, and whether the determination would be just in light of the remaining issues proceeding to trial.
The court will grant partial summary judgment only if the issue in question has no factual component and can be resolved purely as a question of law.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(3), the court has a discretion to grant partial summary judgment. Post-Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7, courts have recognised — including in cases such as Baywood Homes Partnership v Haditaghi — that partial summary judgment is not always appropriate even where the moving party satisfies the test on the particular issue. The primary consideration is whether granting partial summary judgment would fragment the proceedings, increase costs, cause unnecessary delay, or produce inconsistent findings at the subsequent trial on the remaining issues. Partial summary judgment is appropriate only when the severable issue can be resolved cleanly without prejudicing the integrity and efficiency of the proceedings as a whole.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.04(3); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Partial Summary Judgment

text:
Zamorski Investment Group Inc. is examined for discovery in a securities fraud action brought by Leclerc Pension Fund Ltd. During the examination, the representative for Zamorski gives an undertaking to obtain and produce a particular set of trading records. Six weeks later, Zamorski provides a response to the undertaking stating that it has located the records but has decided not to produce them as they are now claimed to be privileged — a privilege claim not raised at the time the undertaking was given.

Can Zamorski legitimately refuse to produce documents subject to an undertaking by claiming privilege after the undertaking was given?
opts: Yes — a party may always raise a new privilege claim over documents at any time before trial, even after giving an undertaking to produce them.
No — once an undertaking is given, the party is absolutely bound to produce the documents regardless of any subsequently identified privilege.

Generally no — where an undertaking to produce is given without privilege being reserved at the time, raising privilege afterward may be seen as a breach of the undertaking and the opposing party may bring a motion to compel; however, the court retains discretion to consider a genuine privilege claim raised after the undertaking if the circumstances warrant.
Yes — privilege claims supersede all discovery undertakings and the party may raise them without consequence at any point before the documents are actually produced.
expl: Under Ontario discovery practice, an undertaking to produce a document creates a binding obligation. Where privilege was not reserved at the time the undertaking was given, subsequently raising a privilege claim is problematic and may be treated by the court as a breach of the undertaking or as an improper attempt to resile from a commitment. The examining party may bring a motion to compel compliance under r. 31.08. Courts have discretion to permit a genuinely meritorious privilege claim raised after an undertaking — particularly where the privilege was overlooked in good faith — but they will not automatically permit a late privilege claim that appears tactical. The undertaking cannot simply be ignored; the party must explain itself and the court will assess whether the claim is genuine.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 31.07; r. 31.08
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Examination for Discovery | Refusals and Undertakings at Discovery

text:
Takeshi slips and falls at a grocery store on April 15, 2024 and sustains personal injuries. He decides to consult a lawyer on November 1, 2025.

What is the last day Takeshi may commence a proceeding for his personal injury claim, assuming he discovered his claim on the date of the fall?
opts: April 15, 2026
November 1, 2027
April 15, 2025
There is no limitation period for personal injury claims in Ontario
expl: Under s. 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002, a proceeding must be commenced within two years of the day the claim was discovered. Takeshi discovered his claim on April 15, 2024 (the date of the fall), so the limitation period expires on April 15, 2026. His decision to consult a lawyer on November 1, 2025 does not affect the calculation.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period

text:
During examination for discovery, the defendant's counsel gives several undertakings to produce documents and provide additional information after the examination. After the examination is complete, new information arises that contradicts one of the undertakings that was given.

What obligation does a party have with respect to undertakings given at examination for discovery?
opts: Undertakings are voluntary commitments; a party may decline to honour them if circumstances change after the examination.

Undertakings given at examination for discovery are binding obligations that must be fulfilled within the time agreed or, if not agreed, within a reasonable time; failure to comply may result in a motion for an order compelling compliance.
An undertaking becomes void if subsequent information contradicts the undertaking, releasing the party from its obligation.
Undertakings are binding only if they were confirmed in writing and signed by counsel after the examination.
expl: Undertakings given at examination for discovery are binding obligations on the party that gave them. They must be fulfilled within the time agreed at the discovery, or within a reasonable time if no deadline was set. Failure to honour undertakings is a serious procedural default that can result in a motion to compel, costs sanctions, or other remedies. If subsequent information contradicts an undertaking, the party must disclose this contradiction and comply with its underlying obligation — the subsequent information does not void the undertaking.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.06; discovery undertakings practice
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Examination for Discovery | Scope — Relevance to Any Matter in Issue

text:
Nadia underwent abdominal surgery in February 2020. She recovered and had no reason to suspect anything was wrong. In March 2023, she began experiencing severe pain. A specialist informed her in March 2023 that a surgical sponge had been left inside her during the 2020 surgery and was the cause of her pain. She had no reason to suspect a problem before March 2023.

When was Nadia's medical malpractice claim 'discovered' for the purposes of the Limitations Act, 2002?
opts: February 2020, when the surgery was performed and the sponge was left inside her
March 2023, when she first knew that her injury was caused by the surgeon's act or omission
The date the surgical sponge was inserted, because the act occurred then
Six years after the surgery, under the former limitation period for medical malpractice
expl: Under s. 5(1)(b) of the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim is not discovered until the claimant knew or ought to have known that the injury was caused by an act or omission of the defendant. Nadia had no symptoms and no reason to investigate until March 2023. The s. 5(2) presumption (that the claim was discovered on the day of the act) is rebutted because she did not know and could not reasonably have known before March 2023. Discovery therefore occurred in March 2023.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, ss. 5(1), 5(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Discoverability — Section 5

text:
A defendant moves to discharge an interlocutory injunction previously granted ex parte. The plaintiff has demonstrated a serious question to be tried and irreparable harm, but the balance of convenience between the parties is genuinely uncertain — both sides would suffer significant harm depending on the outcome.

What should the court do when assessing the balance of convenience in these circumstances?
opts: Discharge the injunction automatically because the plaintiff has failed to clearly satisfy all three elements.
Maintain the injunction for the plaintiff because the first two elements are satisfied and the balance element cannot override an otherwise valid case.
The court weighs the relative harm to each party from granting or refusing the injunction; where the balance is genuinely equal, courts generally favour maintaining the status quo pending trial.
Refer the question of the injunction to the trial judge for determination at the merits stage.
expl: Under the third branch of the RJR-MacDonald test, the court weighs which party would suffer greater harm from the grant or refusal of the injunction. The court considers all relevant factors. Where the balance of convenience is genuinely equal — neither side would suffer significantly more than the other — courts have recognized that maintaining the status quo pending trial is generally appropriate, as this avoids irreversible changes while the case awaits resolution on its merits.
caseRef: RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 6 — Motions | Interlocutory Injunctions — RJR-MacDonald Test

text:
Fontaine Technologies Inc. is the defendant in a breach of software licence claim brought by Oduya Digital Corp. Fontaine's counsel is served with the summary judgment motion and believes the plaintiff's case is thin. Counsel advises Fontaine to file only its statement of defence as its responding record, reasoning that since the burden of proof lies on the plaintiff, the plaintiff must disprove the defence on the motion and the defendant need not file any evidence unless and until the moving party establishes a prima facie case.

Is counsel's advice to Fontaine correct?
opts: Yes — the plaintiff as moving party carries the entire burden on a summary judgment motion, so the defendant need not file any evidence at this stage.
Yes — the defendant's statement of defence, once filed, constitutes sufficient evidence of the defendant's position for the purposes of a summary judgment motion.

No — while the moving party bears the initial burden of showing there is no genuine issue requiring a trial, the responding party (Fontaine) must then put its best foot forward by filing affidavit or other evidence showing a genuine issue exists; resting on the statement of defence alone violates r. 20.02(2).
No — the defendant is required to serve an expert's report explaining why the plaintiff's claim lacks merit before any summary judgment motion can proceed.
expl: Under r. 20.02(2) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, a party responding to a summary judgment motion may not rest on the mere allegations or denials in its pleadings but must set out, by affidavit or otherwise, specific facts showing that there is a genuine issue requiring a trial. This 'best foot forward' obligation, confirmed in Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7, applies to the responding party once the moving party has met its initial burden. Filing only a statement of defence is a classic exam trap — it violates r. 20.02(2) and risks summary judgment being granted by default for lack of any genuine factual issue shown in the responding record.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 20.02(2); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Summary Judgment | Evidence on Summary Judgment Motion

text:
Przybylski Energy Corp. is involved in a commercial dispute with Nadarajah Pipeline Ltd. During examination for discovery, Przybylski's president voluntarily discloses the general conclusions of a privileged legal opinion — specifically stating that 'counsel's opinion confirmed our contract interpretation was correct' — in order to explain the company's decision-making. Nadarajah subsequently seeks full production of the privileged opinion, arguing that privilege has been waived.

Has Przybylski waived solicitor-client privilege over the full legal opinion by disclosing its general conclusions during the examination for discovery?
opts: No — a party may reveal the conclusions of a privileged opinion without waiving privilege over the document itself; only production of the full document constitutes waiver.
No — privilege is waived only if the disclosure was made in writing and signed by the party's legal counsel.

Yes — by voluntarily disclosing the substance or conclusions of a privileged communication in the course of litigation, Przybylski has impliedly waived privilege over the opinion, entitling Nadarajah to production of the full document.
Yes — but only with respect to the single sentence disclosed; the remainder of the opinion retains privilege.
expl: Under Ontario privilege law, solicitor-client privilege may be waived expressly or impliedly. Implied waiver occurs where a party voluntarily discloses the substance or conclusions of a privileged communication in a manner inconsistent with maintaining the confidentiality of the full document. By stating that counsel's opinion confirmed the company's contract interpretation, Przybylski has disclosed the advice in a way that makes it unfair to shield the underlying opinion from scrutiny. Courts have consistently held that partial disclosure of the substance of a privileged communication in the course of proceedings constitutes implied waiver that extends to the full communication, preventing selective use of privilege as both a shield and a sword.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery

text:
Mensah Corporate Law LLP is advising Osei Manufacturing Inc. on a regulatory compliance matter. Osei's general counsel asks the litigation partner at Mensah for a written legal opinion on whether Osei's current waste disposal practices comply with the Environmental Protection Act. The opinion is marked confidential. Osei is later sued by a neighbouring landowner alleging environmental contamination, and the plaintiff demands production of the legal opinion in the affidavit of documents.

Is the Mensah Legal opinion protected by solicitor-client privilege, and must Osei disclose it in its affidavit of documents?
opts: No — the opinion relates to regulatory compliance, not litigation, so it is only protected by litigation privilege and must be produced once the litigation ends.

Yes — the opinion is protected by solicitor-client privilege because it is a confidential communication between lawyer and client made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice; it must be listed in Schedule B of Osei's affidavit of documents but need not be produced.
No — solicitor-client privilege applies only to communications made during the course of litigation, so pre-litigation legal opinions are not protected.
Yes — the opinion is protected, and Osei is not required to even acknowledge that the opinion exists in its affidavit of documents.
expl: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 30.03(2), documents for which privilege is claimed must be listed in Schedule B of the affidavit of documents — the privilege claim must be made, but the document need not be produced. Solicitor-client privilege attaches to a confidential communication between a lawyer and client made for the purpose of giving or obtaining legal advice. The three elements are satisfied here: (1) the communication is between Mensah (lawyer) and Osei (client); (2) it involves the giving of legal advice on regulatory compliance; and (3) it is marked confidential and intended to be so. Solicitor-client privilege is permanent — it does not expire when litigation ends — and it applies to pre-litigation legal advice, not only to communications made during litigation.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery

text:
Castellano Pharma Inc. employs an in-house legal counsel, Dr. Osei-Bonsu, who holds a law degree and is a member of the Law Society of Ontario. In practice, Dr. Osei-Bonsu divides his time equally between providing legal advice to the executive team and acting as a business strategist on product pricing and commercial negotiations. During document production in a class action, the plaintiff seeks production of emails between the CEO and Dr. Osei-Bonsu discussing pricing strategy decisions in which Dr. Osei-Bonsu occasionally added brief references to regulatory risk.

Are the emails between the CEO and Dr. Osei-Bonsu protected by solicitor-client privilege?
opts: Yes — all communications between a client and an individual who is a member of the Law Society of Ontario are automatically protected by solicitor-client privilege.
No — in-house counsel can never give rise to solicitor-client privilege because they are employees of the client, not independent legal advisors.

It depends — solicitor-client privilege applies only to those portions of the emails where Dr. Osei-Bonsu was acting in his legal capacity giving legal advice; communications where he was acting as a business strategist on pricing and commercial matters are not protected.
Yes — any email that contains even a single reference to regulatory risk qualifies as a communication for the purpose of legal advice, protecting the entire email.
expl: Under the law confirmed in Pritchard v Ontario (Human Rights Commission), 2004 SCC 31 as applied to in-house counsel, solicitor-client privilege applies to communications with in-house counsel only where the lawyer was acting in their legal capacity — giving or seeking legal advice — and the communication was intended to be confidential. Where in-house counsel acts in a business, strategic, or commercial capacity, the communications are not privileged. Courts undertake a document-by-document or communication-by-communication analysis to separate legal advice components from business advice components. The emails where Dr. Osei-Bonsu acted as a business strategist on pricing are not privileged; those where he provided legal advice on regulatory risk may be privileged. A brief regulatory reference embedded in a predominantly business communication does not automatically make the full email privileged.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2); Pritchard v Ontario (Human Rights Commission), 2004 SCC 31
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Documentary Discovery | Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery

text:
Obiageli was injured in a hit-and-run car accident in 2021. She knew immediately that she was injured and that a vehicle had struck her, but the driver fled the scene. Obiageli spent approximately eight months working with police and her insurer trying to identify the driver before she gave up. She finally identified the driver through a witness in July 2023.

When did Obiageli's claim against the driver likely become 'discovered' under s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002?
opts: The day of the accident in 2021, because she knew she was injured and that a vehicle caused the injury
Eight months after the accident, when she stopped investigating, because she ought to have discovered the defendant's identity by then
July 2023, when she actually identified the driver, because s. 5(1)(c) requires knowledge of the identity of the defendant
She has no viable claim because she failed to sue within two years of the accident
expl: Section 5(1)(c) of the Limitations Act, 2002 provides that a claim is not discovered until the claimant knows or ought to know the identity of the defendant. Obiageli could not have commenced proceedings against an unknown defendant. She diligently investigated and identified the driver in July 2023. Under s. 5(1)(c), discovery occurred when she identified the driver. She then has two years from July 2023 to commence her action.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(1)(c)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Discoverability — Section 5

text:
On a summary judgment motion, the motion judge is presented with competing affidavit evidence. The judge prefers the defendant's evidence on a key factual issue and wishes to resolve the dispute without sending it to trial.

Under what circumstances may a motion judge weigh evidence and assess credibility on a summary judgment motion?
opts: A motion judge may never weigh evidence; if there is conflicting evidence on a material fact, the case must proceed to trial.
A motion judge may always weigh evidence and assess credibility on a summary judgment motion without any restriction.
Under r. 20.04(2.1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure, a motion judge may weigh evidence, evaluate credibility, and draw reasonable inferences, but only if doing so would not be unjust.
A motion judge may weigh evidence and assess credibility only if both parties expressly consent to this approach.
expl: Rule 20.04(2.1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure grants motion judges expanded powers on summary judgment, including the ability to weigh evidence, draw reasonable inferences from the evidence, and evaluate credibility of deponents. However, these powers may be exercised only if it would not be unjust to do so. This threshold reflects the Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7 principle that summary judgment should be granted where it can be done fairly and justly, but not at the expense of a party's right to a full hearing on a genuinely contested credibility issue.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 20.04(2.1); Hryniak v Mauldin, 2014 SCC 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 7 — Summary Judgment | No Genuine Issue Requiring a Trial

text:
Farah loaned $25,000 to her sister Leila on January 15, 2020, repayable on demand. Farah made a written demand for repayment on March 1, 2023. Leila did not repay the loan.

What is the last day Farah may commence a court proceeding to recover the loan?
opts: January 15, 2022
March 1, 2025
There is no limitation period because this is a family loan.
January 15, 2025
expl: For demand obligations, the discoverability provisions of s. 5 of the Limitations Act, 2002 operate so that the limitation period begins to run when demand is made (and not paid), because it is only at that point that the claimant knows a proceeding would be an appropriate means to seek to remedy the failure. Farah made demand on March 1, 2023; two years from that date is March 1, 2025.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, ss. 4, 5
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period

text:
The defendant in an action is a corporation. The plaintiff wishes to examine the corporation's CEO at examination for discovery, as the CEO has direct personal knowledge of the disputed contractual transaction. The corporation has designated its Vice-President of Finance as its representative for discovery.

How is examination for discovery of a corporation conducted in Ontario, and may the plaintiff compel examination of the CEO?
opts: The plaintiff may examine any officer or director of the corporation it chooses, because they are all representatives of the defendant.
Only the CEO can be examined because he has the most direct personal knowledge of the disputed transaction.

The corporation designates the officer or employee to be examined on its behalf; the plaintiff does not have the right to choose the representative, although the court may order examination of a specific person if the designated representative is unsuitable.
The plaintiff must examine the corporation through written questions only, and cannot compel an oral examination of any individual.
expl: Under r. 31.03 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, where a party to be examined is a corporation, it shall be examined through an officer, director, or employee designated by the corporation. The plaintiff does not have the right to choose which individual represents the corporation. However, if the designated person does not have knowledge of the relevant matters, the examining party may bring a motion and the court can order that a specific person — such as the CEO — be examined.
caseRef: Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 31.03
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 9 — Examination for Discovery | Scope — Relevance to Any Matter in Issue

text:
Gideon believed he was defrauded by his investment advisor in 2020 when his portfolio lost value through suspicious trades. He consulted a lawyer in 2020 who told him he had no case. Gideon did not investigate further. In 2024, a second lawyer reviewed the same facts and told Gideon he clearly had a strong fraud claim.

Which statement best describes when Gideon's claim against the investment advisor was 'discovered' for the purposes of the Limitations Act, 2002?
opts: 2024, when the second lawyer confirmed he had a viable claim

Likely 2020, because the 'ought to know' standard in s. 5(1) is objective and a reasonable person with Gideon's knowledge would have known enough to commence proceedings, despite the first lawyer's incorrect advice
The date the first lawyer gave incorrect advice, which paused the running of the limitation period
2026, because reliance on a lawyer's advice always suspends the limitation period
expl: The discoverability standard in s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002 includes both a subjective and objective element: the claimant must know or ought to know the relevant facts. The 'ought to know' test is objective — it asks what a reasonable person with the claimant's abilities and circumstances would have known. Gideon was aware of the suspicious trades in 2020. A reasonable person in his position would have known enough to recognize that a proceeding was appropriate. Incorrect legal advice does not toll or suspend the limitation period. Discovery likely occurred in 2020, and the two-year period would have expired in 2022.
caseRef: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Limitation Periods | Discoverability — Section 5

text:
Immediately upon arresting Kwame for fraud, police begin asking him detailed questions about the alleged scheme before saying anything about his right to a lawyer.

Which Charter right has been violated?
opts: Section 7 — the right to life, liberty, and security of the person.
Section 10(b) — the right to retain and instruct counsel without delay and to be informed of that right.
Section 9 — the right against arbitrary detention.
Section 11(c) — the right not to be compelled to be a witness against oneself.
expl: Section 10(b) of the Charter guarantees that everyone who is arrested or detained has the right to retain and instruct counsel without delay and to be informed of that right. The Supreme Court in R v Manninen [1987] 1 SCR 1233 held that police have both an informational duty (to advise of the right) and an implementational duty (to hold off questioning until the person has had a reasonable opportunity to contact counsel). Beginning questioning before providing the s. 10(b) caution violates both duties.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 10(b) — Right to Retain and Instruct Counsel

text:
An accused is charged with first-degree murder. The Crown's evidence includes security footage appearing to show the accused committing the act, a matching DNA profile, and a witness who claims to have seen the accused flee the scene. The killing involved particular cruelty. The accused has strong community ties and no prior record.

Which ground of detention under s. 515(10) is most appropriate, and what factors does the court consider?
opts: Primary ground — persons charged with murder always present a serious flight risk.
Secondary ground — persons charged with murder are presumed to be a danger to the public.

Tertiary ground — s. 515(10)(c) — considering the strength of the prosecution's case, the gravity of the offence, the circumstances of the killing, and the potential sentence; detention may be justified if release would undermine public confidence in the administration of justice.
The court cannot detain an accused charged with first-degree murder without a special statutory hearing separate from a standard bail hearing.
expl: The tertiary ground under s. 515(10)(c) — upheld as constitutional in R v Hall 2002 SCC 64 — permits detention where it is necessary to maintain confidence in the administration of justice. The court must consider: the apparent strength of the prosecution's case, the gravity of the offence, the circumstances surrounding the offence (including any use of violence), and the potential sentence upon conviction. On these facts — overwhelming evidence, first-degree murder, particular cruelty, life imprisonment — the tertiary ground is plainly engaged even though the accused has community ties and no prior record.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(10)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Three Grounds for Pre-Trial Detention

text:
Henri is charged with sexual assault. His defence counsel brings an application seeking production of the complainant's personal diary, her medical records, and her employment records. Counsel argues that all three categories of records are 'relevant' to Henri's defence and should be produced under the O'Connor procedure.

Which statement best describes the correct legal analysis for this production application?
opts: All three categories of records should be produced because they are all likely relevant to the sexual assault trial.

The O'Connor procedure governs all third party record applications in criminal cases, including sexual offence cases; the judge must order production of all three categories if they are likely relevant.
