Because Henri is charged with a sexual offence, ss. 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code govern the application for the diary, medical records, and employment records relating to the complainant; the accused must satisfy the more stringent statutory regime, not O'Connor, and cannot obtain production merely by showing likely relevance.
Because the records are relevant, Henri may obtain them automatically through the Crown's Stinchcombe disclosure obligations.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 278.1, 'record' is defined broadly to include medical, psychiatric, therapeutic counselling, school, social work, employment, personal journals, and records of sexual conduct relating to the complainant. Where an accused is charged with a sexual offence and seeks production of records described in s. 278.1 that relate to the complainant, the stringent two-stage Mills regime under ss. 278.1–278.91 applies — not the O'Connor procedure. Henri's counsel's application under O'Connor is incorrect. Under the Mills regime, at stage one the accused must establish that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness and that production is sought for a proper purpose. Merely asserting general relevance, or relying on myths about sexual assault complainants, will not suffice.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 278.1–278.91; R v Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668; R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Disclosure | Third Party Records — Production to the Defence

text:
Dimitri, a neighbour, observed a man he did not recognise break a car window and take a laptop bag from inside a car parked on a residential street in Mississauga. The incident occurred at 11:00 p.m. Dimitri did not immediately intervene. By 11:10 p.m., Dimitri saw the same man walking back past his driveway and detained him. Dimitri did not call police until 11:45 p.m., keeping the man restrained in his garage for that period. Theft of property from a motor vehicle is a hybrid offence.

Which of the following most accurately describes the legal issues arising from Dimitri's actions?
opts: The arrest was valid under s. 494(1)(a) because Dimitri personally witnessed an indictable offence, and there is no duty to deliver the arrested person to police.

The arrest may be justified under s. 494(1)(b) if Dimitri reasonably believed the man committed an indictable offence and was freshly pursuing him; however, the 35-minute delay before calling police likely violates the s. 494(3) duty to deliver forthwith, potentially rendering the continued detention unlawful.
The arrest is unlawful because s. 494 applies only to property owners and the car was not Dimitri's property.
The arrest was valid without any limit on the duration of detention, because Dimitri acted in good faith.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(1)(b), any person may arrest without warrant a person who, on reasonable grounds, they believe has committed a criminal offence and is escaping and freshly pursued. Dimitri witnessed the offence and may have been engaged in fresh pursuit when he detained the man at 11:10 p.m. However, s. 494(3) imposes a mandatory duty to deliver the arrested person to a peace officer forthwith — meaning without unreasonable delay. Holding the person for 35 minutes before even calling police is unlikely to satisfy this requirement. The citizen's arrest power does not authorise extended private detention, and failure to comply with s. 494(3) may expose Dimitri to liability for unlawful confinement.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(1)(b) and s. 494(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Citizen's Power of Arrest

text:
Police arrest an accused and inform him of his right to counsel. He asks to speak with a specific lawyer. Over the next 45 minutes, officers attempt to reach that lawyer without success. Police do not offer duty counsel as an alternative. They then proceed to question the accused without any consultation with counsel having taken place.

Was s. 10(b) of the Charter satisfied when police proceeded to question the accused after 45 minutes?
opts: Yes — 45 minutes is always a reasonable waiting period under s. 10(b).
Yes — police fulfilled their implementational duty by informing the accused and waiting 45 minutes.
No — police can never question an accused who has not reached their chosen lawyer.

It depends — the accused must be given a reasonable opportunity to reach counsel, including being offered duty counsel as an alternative; failure to offer duty counsel when the chosen lawyer is unavailable likely renders the implementational duty unsatisfied.
expl: The implementational duty under s. 10(b) requires police to hold off questioning until the accused has had a reasonable opportunity to contact counsel. Where the chosen counsel is unavailable, police should offer duty counsel as an alternative. The accused also has a corresponding duty to diligently exercise the right. Simply waiting 45 minutes without offering any alternative and then proceeding is unlikely to satisfy the implementational duty. There is no fixed time limit — reasonableness depends on all circumstances including whether alternatives were offered.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 10(b) — Right to Retain and Instruct Counsel

text:
Ten minutes after a bank robbery, an officer receives a radio dispatch describing the suspect as a heavyset male, approximately 6'1", wearing a blue jacket and grey cap. The officer spots a man matching this description precisely, three blocks from the bank, walking quickly away. The officer has no other information but the description is a close match to the person in front of her.

Does this information meet the standard for a warrantless arrest under s. 495(1) as set out in R v Storrey?
opts: No — the officer must be certain the person committed the offence before arresting without a warrant.
No — a description match alone can never constitute reasonable grounds for a warrantless arrest.

Yes — if the officer has an objectively reasonable belief based on the matching description and surrounding circumstances that the person committed the robbery; R v Storrey requires more than mere suspicion but does not require certainty.
Yes — any suspicion of an indictable offence justifies a warrantless arrest.
expl: R v Storrey [1990] 1 SCR 241 held that a warrantless arrest under s. 495(1) requires the officer to have subjectively reasonable grounds that are also justifiable from an objective standpoint — more than mere suspicion but less than certainty. A close description match, combined with the temporal and geographic proximity to the robbery, can collectively constitute objectively reasonable grounds. The court examines the totality of the circumstances, not whether any single factor alone is conclusive.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 495(1) and s. 495(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Arrest Without Warrant — Peace Officer Powers

text:
Bertrand, a Canadian citizen, is charged with theft over $5,000 (an indictable offence). He has two prior theft convictions. The Crown argues at the bail hearing that the reverse onus under s. 515(6) applies because Bertrand has a lengthy record and is a danger to the community.

Does s. 515(6) of the Criminal Code apply to reverse the onus in Bertrand's bail hearing?
opts: Yes — an accused with multiple prior convictions for the same type of offence is subject to reverse onus at any bail hearing.
No — reverse onus under s. 515(6) does not apply simply because the accused has a criminal record or poses a risk of reoffending; it applies only to the specific categories listed in s. 515(6).
Yes — theft over $5,000 is a serious indictable offence and all serious indictable offences attract reverse onus.
No — reverse onus never applies to property offences under any circumstances.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6), reverse onus applies only in specified circumstances: (a) the accused is charged with an indictable offence while on release for another indictable offence; (b) the accused is a non-citizen charged with an indictable offence; (c) certain terrorism, organized crime, or firearm offences; (d) CDSA trafficking, importing, or production offences. Having a prior criminal record — even multiple convictions for the same type of offence — does not, standing alone, engage the reverse onus. The Crown's proper recourse is to argue the secondary ground (substantial likelihood of reoffending) under s. 515(10)(b) with the ordinary Crown burden.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Bail Hearing — Show Cause Hearing

text:
An officer observes someone shoplifting merchandise valued at $80 from a convenience store (theft under $5,000, a hybrid offence). The person immediately provides valid government-issued identification, lives in the neighbourhood, has no criminal record, and agrees in writing to appear in court. The officer has no reason to believe the person will continue offending, destroy evidence, or flee.

What does s. 495(2) of the Criminal Code require the officer to do in this situation?
opts: The officer may arrest because the person committed a criminal offence.
The officer shall not arrest; s. 495(2) prohibits arrest where no ground from the enumerated list is present for a hybrid or summary offence.
The officer must arrest and then release the person on a recognizance at the station.
The officer may arrest at discretion regardless of s. 495(2).
expl: Section 495(2) of the Criminal Code mandates that a peace officer shall not arrest without warrant a person who has committed only a summary conviction offence or a hybrid offence (where Crown has not yet elected) unless arrest is necessary to: (i) establish the person's identity, (ii) secure or preserve evidence, (iii) prevent continuation or repetition of the offence, or (iv) ensure court appearance. Because none of those grounds exist on these facts — identity is confirmed, appearance agreed to, no evidence risk — the officer is prohibited from arresting and should instead issue an appearance notice or obtain a summons.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 495(1) and s. 495(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Arrest Without Warrant — Peace Officer Powers

text:
Crown complied fully with its initial Stinchcombe disclosure obligation. Two months before trial, the police laboratory completes a DNA analysis that was not yet available at the time of initial disclosure. The DNA results actually exculpate the accused. Crown does not provide this new report to defence until the morning of the first day of trial.

Has Crown violated its disclosure obligations by waiting until the day of trial to disclose the new DNA evidence?
opts: No — once initial disclosure is complete, Crown has discharged its obligation and has no further duty to disclose.
No — evidence obtained after the initial disclosure is exempt from the continuing duty under Stinchcombe.

Yes — R v Stinchcombe imposes a continuing duty to disclose; the Crown must disclose new relevant evidence promptly as it is obtained, and waiting until the day of trial to disclose exculpatory DNA evidence may prejudice the accused's right to make full answer and defence.
No — Crown has a discretion to time the disclosure of forensic reports and may choose to disclose on the first day of trial.
expl: Stinchcombe held that the disclosure duty is explicitly continuing — it does not end with the initial disclosure package. As new relevant information becomes available, it must be disclosed in a timely manner. Waiting two months to disclose exculpatory DNA evidence until the morning of trial is a breach of the continuing duty. Such late disclosure may deprive defence of the opportunity to properly investigate, reconsider the accused's election and plea, or seek a stay of proceedings. Crown has no discretion to strategically time the disclosure of relevant evidence.
caseRef: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles

text:
Geneviève is a Canadian citizen charged with robbery, an indictable offence. She has an extensive criminal record including two prior robbery convictions and four prior breaches of probation. She is not currently on release for any other offence at the time of the new robbery charge. The Crown seeks to invoke the reverse onus under s. 515(6).

Does the reverse onus under s. 515(6) of the Criminal Code apply to Geneviève's bail hearing?
opts: Yes — an extensive prior record for the same type of offence automatically triggers the reverse onus under s. 515(6).
Yes — multiple prior breaches of probation are the equivalent of being on release for a prior offence, engaging s. 515(6)(a).

No — Geneviève is not currently on release for another indictable offence, is not a non-citizen, and the robbery charge does not fall within any other s. 515(6) category; the ordinary show cause burden rests with the Crown.
Yes — robbery is a serious violent offence and all violent indictable offences carry reverse onus.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6), the reverse onus is triggered only in enumerated circumstances. Section 515(6)(a) requires the accused to be on release in respect of a prior indictable offence at the time of the new charge — Geneviève is not. Section 515(6)(b) applies to non-citizens — Geneviève is a Canadian citizen. No other category in s. 515(6) applies. A prior record, including prior robbery convictions and probation breaches, does not independently trigger the reverse onus. The Crown's remedy is to make its case under the secondary ground (s. 515(10)(b)) with the ordinary burden on the Crown. This is a classic exam trap: an extensive record does not substitute for the statutory trigger conditions.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Bail Hearing — Show Cause Hearing

text:
At a bail hearing for an accused charged with fraud over $5,000, defence counsel argues for release on the accused's own recognizance with no conditions. The Crown requests a $10,000 cash deposit and a number of stringent conditions including a curfew and electronic monitoring.

What principle governs how a justice approaches the setting of bail conditions?
opts: The court must always require a cash deposit for charges of moderate seriousness.
The ladder principle applies: the court begins with the least restrictive form of release and escalates only as necessary to address the relevant ground for detention.
Once the Crown requests a cash deposit, the court cannot impose a lesser condition.
The court must accept whichever conditions the Crown proposes as long as the Crown has filed the request in writing.
expl: The ladder principle (also referred to as the principle of escalating conditions) requires a justice to begin at the lowest rung of the release ladder — release on one's own recognizance with no conditions — and move to more restrictive forms of release only to the extent necessary to address the relevant ground for detention. The court retains full discretion and is not bound by the Crown's proposed conditions. Cash deposits and stringent conditions are available but should be imposed only where lesser measures are insufficient.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Three Grounds for Pre-Trial Detention

text:
A suspect is detained and invokes the right to counsel. She speaks with duty counsel for about 10 minutes and is then placed in an interview room. Police begin questioning her about a minor theft. Midway through the interview, the detective shifts the questioning and asks her about a homicide that occurred the previous night, for which she is now a suspect.

Does the shift from questioning about the minor theft to questioning about a homicide re-trigger the accused's right to counsel under s. 10(b)?
opts: No — once the accused has spoken with counsel, the right to counsel is fully satisfied for the entire duration of the interrogation.
No — police may change the subject matter of questioning at will without re-triggering the right to counsel.

Yes — under R v Sinclair, 2010 SCC 35, a material change in the accused's jeopardy — such as shifting from a minor theft to a homicide — constitutes a new circumstance that re-triggers the right to counsel.
Only if the accused specifically requests a second consultation with counsel at that moment.
expl: R v Sinclair 2010 SCC 35 held that while s. 10(b) does not provide an ongoing right to have counsel present during interrogation, the right can be re-triggered by a change in circumstances. A material change in the nature or extent of the accused's jeopardy — such as moving from questioning about a minor theft to a homicide — is precisely the kind of changed circumstance that requires police to re-advise and provide a renewed opportunity to consult counsel before continuing.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 10(b) — Right to Retain and Instruct Counsel

text:
A police officer on patrol witnesses the accused punch a victim in a public park. The offence of assault is a hybrid offence. The officer did not obtain a warrant prior to the incident.

May the officer arrest the accused without a warrant?
opts: No — the officer needs a warrant to arrest for a hybrid offence.
Yes — s. 495(1)(b) of the Criminal Code permits a peace officer to arrest without warrant a person found committing a criminal offence.
Only if the Crown has already elected to proceed by indictment.
Only for indictable offences committed in the officer's presence.
expl: Section 495(1)(b) of the Criminal Code authorizes a peace officer to arrest without a warrant a person who is found committing a criminal offence. The power applies to any criminal offence — indictable, summary, or hybrid — when the officer directly witnesses the offence being committed. No warrant is required, and no prior Crown election is necessary.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 495(1) and s. 495(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Arrest Without Warrant — Peace Officer Powers

text:
Police arrest Omar at the front door of his apartment for fraud. After handcuffing him, officers enter the apartment and conduct a thorough search of the bedroom, kitchen, and bathroom — rooms at some distance from where Omar was arrested. They do not have a search warrant. They find documents in the bedroom relevant to the fraud charges.

Were the officers entitled under the search incident to arrest doctrine to search Omar's bedroom, kitchen, and bathroom?
opts: Yes — SITA extends throughout the entire premises where the arrest occurs.
Yes — once a person is arrested inside or at their home, SITA authorises a protective sweep of all rooms.

No — SITA is limited to a search of the person and the area within their immediate control at the time of arrest; searching additional rooms in a dwelling requires a warrant, except to the extent safety concerns justify a limited protective sweep.
No — SITA cannot apply to a dwelling house under any circumstances.
expl: Under Cloutier v Langlois [1990] 1 SCR 158, search incident to arrest is limited to a search of the person and the area within their immediate control at the time of arrest. The rationale is officer safety, evidence preservation, and discovery of evidence related to the offence. Searching the bedroom, kitchen, and bathroom of a person arrested at the front door goes far beyond the area of immediate control and exceeds the scope of SITA. A warrant is required to search those rooms. A limited protective sweep may be permissible for safety reasons, but a full evidentiary search of the entire dwelling is not justified by SITA.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; Cloutier v Langlois, [1990] 1 SCR 158
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Incident to Arrest

text:
At 1:30 a.m., Sergeant Wozniak observes Nathaniel walking quickly through an area known for recent residential break-ins. Nathaniel is wearing a dark hoodie and carrying a backpack. The sergeant pulls his cruiser alongside Nathaniel, activates the vehicle's overhead lights, exits the vehicle, and says, 'Stop. I need to talk to you.' Nathaniel stops. Sergeant Wozniak does not have grounds to arrest Nathaniel.

Which of the following best characterises this encounter under s. 9 of the Charter?
opts: No detention has occurred because Sergeant Wozniak has not announced an arrest.
A lawful investigative detention exists, provided Sergeant Wozniak had reasonable grounds to suspect Nathaniel was connected to a recent or ongoing criminal offence, per R v Mann.
Any detention in this circumstance is arbitrary because the sergeant has no grounds whatsoever and nighttime walking is not suspicious.
A lawful detention exists because activated police lights always constitute lawful authority to detain pedestrians.
expl: Under R v Mann, 2004 SCC 52, police may detain a person briefly for investigative purposes where they have reasonable grounds to suspect the person is connected to a recent or ongoing criminal offence. This is a lower standard than the reasonable grounds to believe required for arrest. The encounter here clearly constitutes a detention — the overhead lights, exit from the vehicle, and command 'Stop' would lead a reasonable person to conclude there was no realistic choice but to comply (per Grant). Whether the detention is lawful turns on whether the sergeant had the Mann standard of reasonable suspicion: walking quickly at 1:30 a.m. in dark clothing through a break-in-prone area provides potential reasonable suspicion, but the question expressly requires application of the Mann test.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 9; R v Mann, 2004 SCC 52; R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 9 — Right Against Arbitrary Detention

text:
An officer lawfully arrests a suspect for trafficking in cocaine. Invoking search incident to arrest, the officer seizes the suspect's locked smartphone at the scene and, without a warrant, conducts an extensive review of the phone's text messages, photos, and contacts over the following hour. This search reveals a text message network used to coordinate drug sales.

What does R v Fearon say about the validity of this cell phone search as a search incident to arrest?
opts: SITA cannot apply to cell phones in any circumstances; a warrant is always required to search a phone.
SITA applies to cell phones on the same basis as any other item seized; a comprehensive search is permissible.

Under R v Fearon, SITA of a cell phone is permitted only where the search is truly incidental to the arrest, involves minimal intrusion, and police promptly record the reasons for the search and what was examined.
SITA of a cell phone is lawful only if the accused consents to the search.
expl: R v Fearon 2014 SCC 77 held that SITA can apply to cell phones but subject to stricter constraints than apply to physical items, given the volume and sensitivity of personal information phones contain. The search must be: (1) truly incidental to the arrest — i.e., undertaken to find evidence related to the offence of arrest; (2) limited in scope to what is genuinely necessary; and (3) accompanied by detailed contemporaneous notes of what was searched, why, and what was found. An extensive hour-long review without documentation likely exceeds the permissible scope under Fearon.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 8 — Unreasonable Search and Seizure

text:
The Crown seeks to detain an accused who has been charged with multiple counts of sexual assault. The Crown presents evidence that the accused has a prior conviction for a similar offence and the psychological report tendered indicates an ongoing risk of reoffending. The Crown argues the accused should be detained to protect potential future victims.

Which ground of detention applies and what must the Crown establish?
opts: Primary ground — there is a risk the accused will flee the jurisdiction before trial.

Secondary ground — under s. 515(10)(b), the Crown must establish a substantial likelihood that the accused, if released, will commit a criminal offence or will interfere with the administration of justice.
Tertiary ground — because sexual assault is a particularly serious offence, detention is automatically justified.
No ground applies — bail cannot be refused for sexual assault until the accused is convicted.
expl: Section 515(10)(b) — the secondary ground — permits detention where there is a substantial likelihood that the accused will commit a criminal offence or interfere with the administration of justice if released. The Crown must establish this on a substantial likelihood standard — not mere possibility. The prior conviction for a similar offence and the psychological risk assessment directly support the secondary ground by demonstrating an ongoing danger to the public.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(10)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Three Grounds for Pre-Trial Detention

text:
Priya is walking through a Toronto park when she witnesses a man punch another man in the face, knocking him to the ground. The aggressor begins to walk away. Priya grabs the aggressor's arm and holds him until police arrive five minutes later. Assault causing bodily harm is a hybrid offence.

Under s. 494(1)(a) of the Criminal Code, did Priya have the legal authority to make a citizen's arrest?
opts: Yes — s. 494(1)(a) allows any person to arrest someone they find committing an indictable offence, and assault causing bodily harm can be prosecuted as an indictable offence.
No — citizen's arrests under s. 494(1)(a) are restricted to offences that are pure indictable offences and never apply to hybrid offences.
Yes — but only if Priya holds a valid first aid certification at the time of the arrest.
No — citizen's arrest powers are exclusively available to property owners whose property is being targeted.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(1)(a), any person may arrest without warrant a person whom they find committing an indictable offence. Assault causing bodily harm is a hybrid offence that may be proceeded with by indictment. Where a hybrid offence is being committed and is capable of being prosecuted as an indictable offence, s. 494(1)(a) is engaged. Priya found the aggressor in the act of committing the offence and acted immediately, satisfying the 'finds committing' requirement. There is no licensing requirement, and the power extends well beyond property owners.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(1)(a)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Citizen's Power of Arrest

text:
Fatima has been charged with trafficking in a controlled substance contrary to s. 5 of the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act. She has no prior criminal record. At her bail hearing, the justice tells defence counsel that it is Fatima's burden to show why she should be released.

Is the justice correct that Fatima bears the burden at this bail hearing?
opts: No — the Crown always bears the burden of showing cause for detention in all criminal matters, including drug trafficking charges.
Yes — under s. 515(6) of the Criminal Code, a person charged with trafficking under s. 5 of the CDSA bears the burden of showing cause why their detention is not justified.
No — reverse onus only applies if Fatima was previously convicted of a drug offence.
Yes — reverse onus applies to all serious criminal charges regardless of the specific offence.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6), the accused must show cause why detention is not justified in specific circumstances. Section 515(6)(d) applies where the accused is charged with an offence under s. 5 (trafficking), s. 6 (importing/exporting), or s. 7 (production) of the Controlled Drugs and Substances Act. The reverse onus applies regardless of the accused's prior record. Fatima is charged with trafficking under s. 5 CDSA, which squarely engages s. 515(6), and the justice is correct to impose the burden on Fatima.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6)(d); Controlled Drugs and Substances Act, S.C. 1996, c. 19, s. 5
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Bail Hearing — Show Cause Hearing

text:
Officers set up a RIDE checkpoint on a Friday night in London, Ontario. They wave every third vehicle over for a brief roadside sobriety check. Leonora is waved over and asked standard questions about whether she has been drinking. She has consumed no alcohol. The stop lasts 90 seconds and she is permitted to leave.

Does stopping Leonora at the RIDE checkpoint constitute an arbitrary detention under s. 9 of the Charter?
opts: Yes — stopping a vehicle without any articulable cause is always arbitrary under s. 9.
No — random roadside stops for sobriety checks are authorised by provincial highway traffic legislation and have been upheld as a reasonable limit on s. 9 rights under s. 1 of the Charter.
No — because Leonora had not consumed alcohol, no Charter right could have been engaged.
Yes — RIDE programs violate s. 9 unless the driver is actually impaired.
expl: Under R v Hufsky [1988] 1 SCR 621 and R v Ladouceur [1990] 1 SCR 1257, random vehicle stops do constitute a detention under s. 9 because drivers have no realistic choice but to comply when directed by police. However, random stops conducted pursuant to provincial highway traffic legislation — such as RIDE programs — are authorised by law and have been held to constitute a reasonable limit under s. 1 of the Charter. The compelling governmental interest in reducing impaired driving justifies this modest and systematic intrusion.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, ss. 9 and 1; R v Hufsky, [1988] 1 SCR 621; R v Ladouceur, [1990] 1 SCR 1257
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 9 — Right Against Arbitrary Detention

text:
Police arrest a suspect and read a standard s. 10(b) caution advising of the right to retain and instruct counsel. The caution does not mention Legal Aid or duty counsel. The suspect, who cannot afford a private lawyer, says nothing and does not invoke the right. Police proceed with questioning.

Has the informational component of s. 10(b) been fully complied with?
opts: Yes — s. 10(b) only requires informing the accused of the right to retain and instruct counsel; there is no duty to mention Legal Aid.
No — under R v Brydges, police must inform detained persons of the availability of Legal Aid and duty counsel programs; failure to do so violates the informational component of s. 10(b).
No — s. 10(b) requires that the accused be provided with a lawyer at government expense immediately upon request.
Yes — the duty to inform of Legal Aid only arises if the accused expressly states they cannot afford a lawyer.
expl: R v Brydges [1990] 1 SCR 190 expanded the informational duty under s. 10(b) to require that police inform detained persons of the availability of Legal Aid and duty counsel. This obligation is proactive and does not depend on the accused asking or disclosing financial need. The standard caution that omits reference to these programs is therefore incomplete and constitutes a breach of the informational component of s. 10(b).
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 10(b) — Right to Retain and Instruct Counsel

text:
Konstantin, a German national temporarily in Canada on a work permit, is arrested and charged with assault causing bodily harm, a hybrid offence. He has been in Canada for two years, has steady employment, and owns a condominium in Toronto. The Crown argues that because Konstantin is a non-citizen charged with an indictable offence, the reverse onus under s. 515(6)(b) applies.

Does the reverse onus under s. 515(6)(b) apply to Konstantin's bail hearing, and if so, what must he demonstrate?
opts: No — s. 515(6)(b) applies only to persons who are in Canada illegally, not those with valid work permits.

Yes — under s. 515(6)(b), a person who is not a Canadian citizen and who is charged with an indictable offence must show cause why their detention is not justified; Konstantin must establish that his detention is not warranted.
No — reverse onus under s. 515(6)(b) applies only when the offence is purely indictable and assault causing bodily harm is a hybrid offence.
Yes — but only if the Crown can first prove that Konstantin intends to leave Canada before trial.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6)(b), where an accused who is not a Canadian citizen is charged with an indictable offence, the accused must show cause why their detention is not justified. Assault causing bodily harm is a hybrid offence that may be prosecuted by indictment, and the reverse onus applies when it is proceeded with as an indictable offence. Konstantin's immigration status as a work permit holder does not exempt him from s. 515(6)(b); the provision applies to non-citizens generally, not only those without legal status. Konstantin must affirmatively show cause why release is appropriate.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6)(b)

text:
Officer Trudeau stops Anaya, a 22-year-old Black woman, while she is walking in a residential neighbourhood in Windsor. Officer Trudeau tells her he is detaining her for investigative purposes because the neighbourhood has had thefts and she 'looks out of place.' He has no other information about Anaya and she has no criminal record. After 15 minutes of questioning, she is released without charge.

Was Anaya's detention under s. 9 of the Charter arbitrary?
opts: No — the officer had reasonable suspicion based on the general crime rate in the neighbourhood, which satisfies the Mann standard.
No — investigative detentions are always lawful provided they last less than 30 minutes.

Yes — under R v Mann, reasonable suspicion must be based on particularized conduct or information connecting the specific individual to a recent or ongoing offence; looking 'out of place' in a general high-crime area without more does not meet the required standard and the detention is arbitrary.
Yes — but only if Anaya can prove that the detention was racially motivated.
expl: Under R v Mann, 2004 SCC 52, investigative detention requires reasonable grounds to suspect that the individual is connected to a recent or ongoing criminal offence. This is not a low threshold — it must be based on particularized information about the specific individual, not a general neighbourhood crime rate. Merely 'looking out of place' in an area with thefts provides no particularized basis to suspect Anaya of wrongdoing. The Supreme Court has consistently warned against allowing vague or discriminatory assessments to substitute for genuine reasonable suspicion. The detention was arbitrary within s. 9. It is also worth noting that 'carding'-style stops raise serious concerns under the Charter's equality guarantees, though Anaya need not prove racial motivation to establish an arbitrary detention under s. 9.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 9; R v Mann, 2004 SCC 52
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 9 — Right Against Arbitrary Detention

text:
Officers arrest Ingrid for shoplifting (theft under $5,000). Purportedly invoking SITA, they seize her unlocked smartphone and, at the police station two hours after arrest, conduct a full review of her photos, emails, and banking applications. They find no evidence related to the shoplifting but discover communications that link her to an unrelated drug operation. They seek to use this as evidence in a new drug trafficking prosecution.

Under R v Fearon and the general SITA doctrine, which of the following best describes the legality of the cell phone search?
opts: The search is valid because SITA authorized the seizure of the phone, and any evidence found during a search of a lawfully seized item is admissible.
The search is valid because the phone was unlocked, removing any reasonable expectation of privacy.

The search violates s. 8 of the Charter on multiple grounds: it was not conducted promptly at the time of arrest, it was a full data review rather than a targeted search, and it was not truly incidental to the shoplifting arrest; the drug evidence should be excluded under s. 24(2).
The search is valid because the evidence discovered, while relating to a different offence, was found during a lawful SITA search.
expl: Under R v Fearon, 2014 SCC 77, a cell phone search incident to arrest must meet all of the following: (1) the arrest must be lawful; (2) the search must be truly incidental to the arrest — connected to the purpose of the arrest; (3) the search must be limited in scope and proportionate; and (4) police must make detailed contemporaneous notes. On these facts, the search fails on multiple grounds. It occurred two hours after arrest (not contemporaneous), it was a comprehensive review of all phone data (not limited), and it was not truly incidental to a shoplifting arrest (no connection between shoplifting and emails or banking apps). The discovery of unrelated drug evidence does not cure these defects. The cell phone search violates s. 8, and the drug evidence should be assessed under the s. 24(2) exclusionary remedy.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; R v Fearon, 2014 SCC 77
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Incident to Arrest

text:
Police execute a valid search warrant for stolen electronics at a private residence. While searching in plain view within the living room — the area specified by the warrant — an officer sees an unsealed bag containing what appears to be heroin sitting on a coffee table. The heroin was not mentioned in the warrant. The officer seizes the bag.

Is the seizure of the heroin admissible, and does it violate s. 8 of the Charter?
opts: No — items not specifically listed in a search warrant are always inadmissible and the seizure violates s. 8.
No — the warrant only authorises seizure of electronics; seizing anything else requires a new warrant.
Yes — any item discovered during a lawful search is automatically admissible without further analysis.

Likely yes — the plain view doctrine permits seizure of items not listed in a warrant when the officer is lawfully in the area, the item is in plain view, and there are reasonable grounds to believe it is evidence of a crime.
expl: The plain view doctrine is a recognized exception under Canadian law that permits officers to seize items not listed in a warrant where: (1) the officer is lawfully in the area where the item is observed (execution of a valid warrant satisfies this); (2) the incriminating character of the item is immediately apparent; and (3) the officer has lawful authority to access the item. An unsealed bag of heroin sitting in plain view during lawful execution of a warrant satisfies these conditions. The seizure would not violate s. 8 in these circumstances.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 8 — Unreasonable Search and Seizure

text:
Marcus manages a convenience store in Hamilton. He observes a woman conceal a chocolate bar (value $3.50) in her jacket pocket and walk past the cash register without paying. The offence is theft under $5,000, which is a hybrid offence. Marcus blocks the exit and detains her. He then calls the police, who arrive 20 minutes later. Marcus does not attempt to deliver her to police any sooner.

Which of the following best describes the legal validity of Marcus's citizen's arrest?
opts: The arrest was unlawful because theft under $5,000 is not an indictable offence and s. 494(1)(a) requires the offence to be indictable.

The arrest was lawful under s. 494(2) because Marcus, as a person authorised by the property owner to manage the premises, found a person committing a criminal offence on or in relation to that property; however, Marcus was also obligated under s. 494(3) to deliver her to police forthwith.
The arrest was unlawful because citizen's arrests can only be made by the property owner personally, not by an employee or manager.
The arrest was lawful under s. 494(1)(a) because Marcus personally witnessed a criminal offence being committed in his presence.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(2), a person who owns property or a person authorized by an owner may arrest without warrant a person whom they find committing any criminal offence — including summary conviction and hybrid offences — on or in relation to that property. A store manager is a person authorized by the owner, and theft occurring in the store is a criminal offence on or in relation to that property. Section 494(1)(a) would not apply independently here because that provision requires an indictable offence. Section 494(3) imposes a duty to deliver the arrested person to a peace officer forthwith, meaning without unreasonable delay.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(2) and s. 494(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Citizen's Power of Arrest

text:
Dominique is charged with sexual assault. Her defence counsel believes that the complainant's psychiatric records, which are held by a therapist and not by the Crown, may contain statements relevant to the complainant's credibility. Defence applies for production of those records to the court.

Which legal regime governs defence access to the complainant's psychiatric records in a sexual assault case?
opts: Stinchcombe — because all relevant third party records must be disclosed by the Crown to the defence.
The O'Connor procedure — because all third party records in criminal proceedings are governed by R v O'Connor regardless of the nature of the offence.

Sections 278.1 to 278.91 of the Criminal Code (the Mills regime) — because these provisions specifically govern production of records relating to complainants in sexual offence proceedings and impose a stringent two-stage process.
The civil rules of evidence — because the records are held by a private third party and not by the Crown.
expl: Under Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 278.1–278.91, Parliament enacted a specific statutory regime — often called the Mills regime — to govern production of records held by third parties relating to complainants in sexual offence proceedings. This regime was upheld in R v Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668. The accused must establish at stage one that the records are likely relevant to an issue at trial or to the competence of a witness, and must show the records are sought for a proper purpose (not relying on rape myths). The regime is more stringent than O'Connor and applies exclusively to complainant records in sexual offence cases.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, ss. 278.1–278.91; R v Mills, [1999] 3 SCR 668
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Disclosure | Third Party Records — Production to the Defence

text:
Police used a confidential informant to gather evidence leading to the accused's arrest for drug trafficking. The informant's tip was central to obtaining the search warrant. Defence brings a Stinchcombe application seeking full disclosure of all information about the informant, including identity.

How does informer privilege interact with Crown's Stinchcombe duty to disclose?
opts: Stinchcombe always prevails; the Crown must disclose all relevant information including the identity of any confidential informant.

Informer privilege is a near-absolute common law privilege; the identity of a confidential informant need not be disclosed even if such information is relevant, because the privilege protects informants and the public interest in law enforcement.
Informant identity is protected for 30 days following the relevant proceedings; after that period, Stinchcombe requires full disclosure.
Defence may always obtain informant identity disclosure by successfully bringing a Stinchcombe application.
expl: Informer privilege is one of the recognised exceptions to the Stinchcombe duty to disclose. It is described as a near-absolute privilege at common law, designed to protect the safety of confidential informants and to preserve the public interest in the free flow of information to law enforcement. The Crown is not required to disclose the identity of a confidential informant even where that information is relevant and potentially helpful to the defence. The privilege can be displaced only in the rarest circumstances — notably, the innocence-at-stake exception.
caseRef: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles

text:
Acting solely on a tip from an anonymous neighbour, police break into a private residence without a search warrant to look for stolen electronics. They find nothing of evidentiary value.

Which statement correctly describes the s. 8 Charter requirement for a valid search of a private residence?
opts: A warrantless search is valid as long as the searching officer believes a crime has been committed.
A search is valid if it ultimately produces evidence of a crime, regardless of the manner in which it was conducted.

Generally, a valid search of a private residence requires prior judicial authorization based on reasonable and probable grounds; a warrantless search is presumptively unreasonable under Hunter v Southam.
Police may always search a home without a warrant if they have objectively reasonable grounds to believe evidence is present.
expl: Hunter v Southam Inc [1984] 2 SCR 145 established that the standard for a reasonable search under s. 8 of the Charter ordinarily requires prior judicial authorization — a warrant — based on reasonable and probable grounds established on oath. A warrantless search is presumptively unreasonable, and the Crown bears the burden of rebutting that presumption. An anonymous tip without more falls well short of the grounds needed even if a warrant were sought.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 8 — Unreasonable Search and Seizure

text:
Constables arrest Yolanda at 9:00 p.m. on a street corner in Thunder Bay for possession of cocaine found on her person. She is taken to the station and booked. At 11:30 p.m. — two and a half hours after her arrest — an officer decides to conduct a strip search at the station, citing search incident to arrest. No exigent circumstances were identified. The strip search reveals no additional evidence.

Was the strip search conducted on Yolanda legally valid as a search incident to arrest?
opts: Yes — strip searches may be conducted at the police station at any time following a lawful arrest as a matter of routine.
Yes — the passage of time between arrest and strip search does not affect the validity of SITA.

No — under R v Golden, 2001 SCC 83, strip searches must not be conducted routinely; they require reasonable and probable grounds that the person is concealing evidence or weapons, and must be conducted in a manner that respects the person's dignity, generally at the police station.
No — strip searches can only be performed by the arresting officer and not by officers at the station.
expl: Under R v Golden, 2001 SCC 83, the Supreme Court held that strip searches incident to arrest are not routine and cannot be conducted as a matter of course. They require: (1) reasonable and probable grounds to believe the strip search is necessary to discover weapons or evidence; (2) to be conducted in a manner that respects dignity; and (3) generally to be performed at the police station rather than at the scene, except in exigent circumstances. Conducting a strip search two and a half hours after arrest with no identified grounds and no exigent circumstances violates Golden and infringes s. 8.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; R v Golden, 2001 SCC 83
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Incident to Arrest

text:
A store owner watches a shoplifter take merchandise and leave the store. By the time the store owner can react, the shoplifter has walked half a block away and is still visible. The store owner runs after and catches the shoplifter on the public sidewalk, detaining him until police arrive.

Under s. 494 of the Criminal Code, was the store owner's citizen's arrest lawful?
opts: No — only peace officers can arrest once an offender has left the scene.
No — the right of citizen's arrest expires the moment the offender leaves the property.

Yes — under s. 494(2), an owner or person in lawful possession of property may arrest a person found committing a criminal offence on or in relation to that property; s. 494(1)(b) also authorizes arrest of a person who has committed an indictable offence and is escaping and freshly pursued.
Yes — but only if the store owner holds a valid security guard licence.
expl: Section 494 of the Criminal Code grants non-peace officers the right to make a citizen's arrest in specific circumstances. Under s. 494(2), an owner of property or a person lawfully in possession of property may arrest a person who they find committing a criminal offence on or in relation to that property. Furthermore, s. 494(1)(b) allows any person to arrest without warrant someone who has committed an indictable offence and is escaping and freshly pursued. The store owner witnessed the shoplifting (a criminal offence on or in relation to the property) and pursued immediately, satisfying both provisions. The person arrested must be delivered to a peace officer without unreasonable delay.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 494(1)(b) and s. 494(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Arrest Without Warrant — Peace Officer Powers

text:
An accused is charged with aggravated assault. Crown provides a package of initial disclosure two weeks before the scheduled election date. Defence counsel argues that disclosure should have been provided earlier and challenges the timing.

When must Stinchcombe disclosure be made to the accused?
opts: Disclosure must be provided at the first court appearance.
Disclosure must be made before the accused is required to elect or enter a plea, and the duty is continuing as new information becomes available.
Disclosure must be provided at least 60 days before the trial date.
Crown may wait until the eve of trial to provide disclosure of all but the most critical evidence.
expl: Stinchcombe held that initial disclosure must be made at the first reasonable opportunity and in any event before the accused is required to elect the mode of trial or enter a plea — because those decisions should be made with knowledge of the evidence. The duty is also explicitly continuing: as new information comes into Crown's possession, it must be disclosed promptly. Providing disclosure two weeks before the election date may be compliant depending on when the election is scheduled, but the core rule is that it must precede election and plea.
caseRef: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles

text:
An officer lawfully arrests a suspect in a public parking lot for drug trafficking. As part of the arrest, and at the scene, the officer searches the suspect's jacket and finds a bag of cocaine in an inside pocket. No warrant was obtained.

Is the warrantless search of the suspect's jacket valid under s. 8 of the Charter?
opts: No — a warrant is required to search a person's clothing even if they have been lawfully arrested.

Yes — under the common law power of search incident to arrest, an officer may search the person arrested and the area within their immediate control; finding cocaine in the jacket falls within this scope.
Only if the officer had independent and specific grounds to believe drugs would be found in the jacket.
Yes, but only if the search was conducted at the police station rather than at the scene of arrest.
expl: Search incident to arrest (SITA) is a well-established common law exception to the s. 8 warrant requirement. When a person is lawfully arrested, police may search the person, their clothing, and items within their immediate control without additional grounds — provided the search is truly incidental to the arrest and not the other way around. A search of the arrested person's jacket at the time and place of arrest plainly falls within the recognized scope of SITA.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 8 — Unreasonable Search and Seizure

text:
Upon arresting Farrukh, Constable Rodriguez reads the standard Charter caution from a card, informing Farrukh of his right to retain and instruct counsel without delay. Farrukh says, 'I understand my rights and I don't need a lawyer. I want to talk.' Police then conduct a full interview. Farrukh later challenges the admissibility of his statement.

Has s. 10(b) of the Charter been complied with?
opts: No — police must still wait a fixed period before questioning even if the accused waives the right to counsel.
Yes — provided Farrukh's waiver of his right to counsel was informed, voluntary, and unequivocal.
No — s. 10(b) requires that an accused actually consult a lawyer before any questioning can occur.
No — a waiver of s. 10(b) rights must be in writing to be legally valid.
expl: The right to counsel under s. 10(b) can be waived by an accused, provided the waiver is informed, voluntary, and unequivocal. Once a valid waiver is established, police are entitled to proceed with questioning. The caution was properly given, and Farrukh expressed clear and unequivocal willingness to speak without a lawyer. There is no fixed mandatory waiting period and no requirement that the accused actually speak with a lawyer before any questioning — what matters is that the opportunity was genuinely afforded and voluntarily declined.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 10(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 10(b) — Right to Retain and Instruct Counsel

text:
An accused has been charged with theft over $5,000. At the bail hearing, the Crown introduces evidence showing the accused failed to appear in court on three separate occasions in the past five years in relation to other charges.

Which ground of detention under s. 515(10) of the Criminal Code is the Crown relying on?
opts: Secondary ground — the likelihood of the accused committing further offences.
Primary ground — detention is necessary to ensure the accused's attendance in court under s. 515(10)(a).
Tertiary ground — maintaining public confidence in the administration of justice.
The Crown cannot rely on prior failures to appear for the purposes of a bail determination.
expl: Section 515(10)(a) of the Criminal Code — the primary ground — permits detention where it is necessary to ensure the accused's attendance in court. A history of failures to appear is directly and squarely relevant to this ground as it demonstrates a pattern of non-compliance with court obligations, suggesting future non-appearance is a genuine risk.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(10)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Three Grounds for Pre-Trial Detention

text:
A police officer receives a tip from an informant with no established track record that a named suspect robbed a bank 30 minutes ago. The officer has no direct evidence, no corroboration, and has never used this informant before. The officer locates the suspect walking two blocks from the bank.

Can the officer arrest the suspect without a warrant based solely on the unvetted tip?
opts: Yes — any information received by an officer can form the basis for a warrantless arrest.
Yes — as long as the officer subjectively believes the informant, the arrest is lawful.

No — a peace officer must have reasonable grounds to believe the person committed an indictable offence; an unvetted tip without corroboration typically does not satisfy the objective standard required by R v Storrey.
No — warrantless arrest for robbery requires the officer to have personally witnessed the offence.
expl: R v Storrey [1990] 1 SCR 241 established that the arresting officer must subjectively have reasonable and probable grounds to believe the suspect committed the offence, and those grounds must be justifiable from an objective standpoint. Mere suspicion is insufficient. A tip from an informant with no track record and no corroborating evidence does not ordinarily rise to the level of objectively reasonable grounds, making a warrantless arrest based solely on that tip unlawful.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 495(1) and s. 495(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Arrest Without Warrant — Peace Officer Powers

text:
Constable Beaumont is on patrol in Ottawa when she notices a man sitting on a park bench at 2:00 a.m. She walks up to him and says, 'Do you mind stopping for a moment and answering some questions?' The man stops and speaks with her voluntarily for ten minutes. He is not told he must stay, and Constable Beaumont does not block his path.

Under R v Grant's framework, has the man been detained within the meaning of s. 9 of the Charter?
opts: Yes — any police contact with a member of the public constitutes detention under the Charter.
No — under R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32, a voluntary encounter where the person has a realistic choice to walk away and police issue no demands does not constitute detention.
Yes — because the encounter occurred late at night and the man would have felt compelled to stop.
Yes — detention occurs whenever a police officer approaches a citizen and asks questions.
expl: Under R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32, the Supreme Court identified three categories of police-public encounters: (1) voluntary interactions with no detention; (2) investigative detentions; and (3) formal arrest. The Grant framework asks whether, objectively, a person in the circumstances would reasonably conclude they had no choice but to comply. Where police make a polite request, do not block the person's path, and the person voluntarily participates, there is no detention. Constable Beaumont's request was framed as optional ('Do you mind'), she imposed no physical constraint, and the person made a free choice to engage. This is a voluntary encounter, not a detention.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 9; R v Grant, 2009 SCC 32
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 9 — Right Against Arbitrary Detention

text:
An accused is currently released on a recognizance for a robbery charge. While on that release, he is arrested and charged with possessing a prohibited firearm, an indictable offence. The Crown seeks detention and argues the accused should be required to show cause why detention is not justified.

Does s. 515(6) of the Criminal Code create a reverse onus on these facts, and if so, what must the accused establish?
opts: No — the Crown always bears the burden of justifying pre-trial detention regardless of circumstances.
No — reverse onus under s. 515(6) applies only to drug trafficking offences.

Yes — under s. 515(6)(a), where an accused is charged with an indictable offence alleged to have been committed while on release for another indictable offence, the accused must show cause why their detention is not justified.
Yes, but only if the previous charge has already resulted in a conviction at the time of the bail hearing.
expl: Section 515(6)(a) of the Criminal Code reverses the ordinary onus at a bail hearing. Where an accused is charged with an indictable offence that is alleged to have been committed while the accused was on release in respect of another indictable offence, the accused bears the burden of showing cause why their detention is not justified. The accused is 'on release' for the robbery charge (an indictable offence), and the prohibited firearm charge is a fresh indictable offence committed during that release — precisely the scenario s. 515(6)(a) addresses.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 515(6)(a)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3: Bail — Judicial Interim Release | Three Grounds for Pre-Trial Detention

text:
During trial, Crown discloses for the first time a key police officer's notebook containing notes that undermine the officer's credibility. Defence had been unaware of the notes. Defence seeks a stay of proceedings on the basis of Crown's non-disclosure. The trial judge notes that the notes can be reviewed overnight and trial can resume the next day.

What is the appropriate remedy for Crown's non-disclosure, and in what circumstances will a stay of proceedings be granted?
opts: A stay of proceedings is the automatic and mandatory remedy for any breach of Crown's Stinchcombe disclosure obligations.
The accused can never obtain a stay for non-disclosure; the only remedy is an adjournment.

The primary remedy is an adjournment to allow the accused to review the disclosed material; a stay of proceedings is reserved for cases where the non-disclosure has caused irreparable prejudice to the accused's right to make full answer and defence.
Crown is immune from any sanction for non-disclosure where the omission was inadvertent rather than deliberate.
expl: The courts have established a spectrum of remedies for Crown non-disclosure. An adjournment to review newly disclosed material is the most common and least drastic remedy, appropriate where the prejudice can be cured by additional time. A stay of proceedings is the most serious Charter remedy and is reserved for cases where the accused has suffered irreparable prejudice to the right to make full answer and defence — prejudice that cannot be cured by any lesser remedy. On these facts, an overnight adjournment appears to cure the prejudice, making a stay inappropriate.
caseRef: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles

text:
Teodora is accused of assaulting her co-worker during a workplace dispute. Her defence counsel believes records from the complainant's employer — including internal investigation reports and disciplinary records — may reveal prior false complaints of workplace violence made by the complainant. These records are not in the Crown's possession.

What procedure must defence follow to obtain these workplace records, and what must be established?
opts: Defence must bring a Stinchcombe application requiring the Crown to obtain the records through the police.
Defence must apply under ss. 278.1–278.91 of the Criminal Code because those provisions apply to all third party records sought in criminal proceedings.

Defence must bring an application under the procedure established in R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, seeking production from the third party; the court will assess likely relevance and balance the accused's right to make full answer and defence against the privacy interests of the complainant.
Defence is not entitled to any third party records held by private employers unless the Crown first discloses them.
expl: The two-stage procedure from R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411, governs production of third party records in non-sexual offence proceedings. Under O'Connor, at the first stage the accused must satisfy the court that the records are likely relevant. If this threshold is met, the records are produced to the judge for review. At the second stage, the judge balances the accused's s. 7 right to make full answer and defence against privacy rights and other competing interests, and determines what, if any, portion should be produced to the accused. The ss. 278.1–278.91 regime does not apply here because this is not a sexual offence case.
caseRef: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46; R v O'Connor, [1995] 4 SCR 411
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Disclosure | Third Party Records — Production to the Defence

text:
Officers arrest Salvatore outside his apartment building in Scarborough for trafficking in heroin. Incident to the arrest, they search his jacket pockets and find a small quantity of cash and a folded piece of paper with what appear to be client names and drug amounts. They also open a locked briefcase he was carrying and search through papers inside. No warrant was obtained for the briefcase.

Which portion of the search was valid as a search incident to arrest?
opts: Both the jacket search and the briefcase search are valid because SITA covers all items in the arrested person's possession.
Neither search is valid because SITA requires a warrant for any items beyond the person's clothing.

Only the jacket pocket search is clearly within the scope of SITA; the briefcase may require a warrant because opening a locked container to search its contents exceeds the immediate safety and evidence preservation rationale and may not be truly incidental to the arrest.
The briefcase search is valid only if its contents ultimately prove relevant to the trafficking charge.
expl: Under Cloutier v Langlois [1990] 1 SCR 158, search incident to arrest authorises a search of the person and the area within their immediate control for purposes of safety, evidence preservation, and discovery of evidence related to the offence. A search of jacket pockets is squarely within this scope. However, opening a locked briefcase and reviewing its contents is a more intrusive search that goes beyond what is immediately apparent or necessary for the core SITA purposes. Courts scrutinise the extension of SITA to locked containers, particularly where the safety rationale is absent and where the search could be deferred pending a warrant.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8; Cloutier v Langlois, [1990] 1 SCR 158
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | Search Incident to Arrest

text:
Detectives have an arrest warrant for a suspect believed to be inside his sister's home. Without seeking any additional judicial authorisation, they enter the home to arrest him. They find him inside and arrest him. The suspect moves to exclude evidence obtained as a result of the entry.

Does the warrantless entry into the dwelling house violate s. 8 of the Charter?
opts: No — an arrest warrant authorises entry into any premises, including a private dwelling, to effect the arrest.
No — reasonable grounds to arrest always justify entry into private premises without additional authorisation.

Yes — under R v Feeney and the subsequent enactment of s. 529.1 of the Criminal Code, entry into a dwelling house to effect an arrest requires specific prior judicial authorisation; a regular arrest warrant does not suffice and warrantless entry absent exigent circumstances violates s. 8.
Yes — but only if the suspect is ultimately acquitted of the offence for which he was arrested.
expl: R v Feeney [1997] 2 SCR 13 held that entry into a private dwelling to effect an arrest requires specific prior judicial authorisation beyond a standard arrest warrant. Following Feeney, Parliament enacted s. 529.1 of the Criminal Code, which authorises a justice to issue a warrant to enter a dwelling house for the purpose of arrest. Entry into a third party's home on the strength of an arrest warrant alone — absent exigent circumstances — is a warrantless entry that violates s. 8.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 8
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2: Charter Rights — Arrest, Detention, and Search | s. 8 — Unreasonable Search and Seizure

text:
The Crown has obtained a signed statement from a witness who says the accused was definitely not at the location where the crime was committed. Crown has decided not to call this witness at trial and has not provided the statement to defence.

Is Crown required to disclose this exculpatory witness statement to defence?
opts: No — the Crown need only disclose evidence it intends to introduce at trial.
Yes — under R v Stinchcombe, the Crown must disclose all relevant information regardless of whether it intends to use it at trial, including exculpatory evidence that may assist the defence.
Only if defence specifically requests the statement in a disclosure application.
No — a witness statement favourable to the accused is protected by litigation privilege.
expl: R v Stinchcombe [1991] 3 SCR 326 established the foundational rule that the Crown must disclose all relevant information in its possession — whether inculpatory or exculpatory — to the defence. The obligation is not limited to evidence Crown intends to use at trial. An exculpatory alibi witness statement is precisely the kind of evidence that must be disclosed because it may assist the accused in making a full answer and defence, as guaranteed by s. 7 of the Charter.
caseRef: R v Stinchcombe, [1991] 3 SCR 326; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4: Disclosure | Crown Duty to Disclose — Stinchcombe Principles

text:
During their marriage, Cheryl's mother gave her a gift of $120,000 with the expectation — stated verbally but never put in writing — that if the marriage ended, the money should be returned to the family. Cheryl deposited the $120,000 into the couple's joint chequing account, where it was mixed with direct-deposit salary and household expenses over several years. On the valuation date, the account holds $15,000. Cheryl's husband Gerald argues the entire $120,000 should be excluded from Cheryl's NFP.

What is the most accurate analysis of whether the $120,000 gift is excluded from Cheryl's NFP?
opts: The $120,000 is excluded in its entirety because the verbal condition attached to the gift means it should be returned and therefore cannot be part of either spouse's NFP.
The $120,000 is excluded in its entirety because all gifts from family members received during the marriage are automatically excluded under FLA s. 4(2)(a) regardless of what happened to them.

The $120,000 gift likely loses its excluded status because it was deposited into a joint account where it commingled with other funds and is no longer traceable; a verbal condition does not separately preserve the exclusion.
The $120,000 is excluded in its entirety because the verbal condition makes it a resulting trust and not a gift at all, so the NFP analysis does not apply.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(a), property received by way of gift from a third party after the date of marriage is excluded from NFP. However, the exclusion requires the property to be traceable at the valuation date. When Cheryl deposited the $120,000 into a joint chequing account where it commingled with salary and household expenses over several years, the funds lost their traceable character. Only $15,000 remains in the account, and it cannot be identified as the gift proceeds. The verbal condition attached to the gift does not satisfy the traceability requirement and does not independently preserve the exclusion.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(a)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property Under FLA s. 4(2)

text:
Renata and her husband Stefan separated after 18 years of marriage. They have two children, aged 10 and 14, for whom Renata has primary residence. The court has found Renata is entitled to both compensatory and non-compensatory spousal support. Stefan's lawyer argues the SSAG without-child formula applies because the parties have negotiated child support separately. Renata's lawyer argues the with-child formula should apply. The court must determine which SSAG formula governs the quantum analysis.

Which SSAG formula applies where the spouses have dependent children in the recipient's primary care?
opts: The without-child formula always applies once child support has been separately determined; the existence of children is irrelevant to the spousal support quantum once child support is resolved.

The with-child formula applies where there are dependent children in the recipient spouse's primary care; this formula accounts for custodial arrangements and typically produces higher spousal support amounts than the without-child formula.
Neither formula applies; courts must set aside the SSAG entirely when children are involved and rely solely on FLA s. 33 factors.
The without-child formula applies because the parties have already negotiated a child support amount and the SSAG formulas are mutually exclusive.
expl: The Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines provide two distinct formulas. The without-child formula applies to marriages without dependent children (or post-secondary adult children). The with-child formula applies where there are dependent children living primarily with the support recipient. The with-child formula is more complex — it takes into account the parenting arrangements, the child support paid, and typically produces a higher range of spousal support to reflect the combined economic impact of child-rearing responsibilities. The fact that child support has been separately agreed does not convert a with-child case into a without-child case; the formula depends on the presence of dependent children in the recipient's primary care.
caseRef: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Spousal Support | Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG)

text:
The parties were married for four years and have no children. Both worked throughout the marriage in similar professional roles. On separation, the husband earns $200,000 per year and the wife earns $190,000 per year. The wife applies for spousal support.

Is the wife entitled to spousal support in these circumstances?
opts: Yes — any spouse may apply for and receive support on separation regardless of need or the nature of the marriage.
Yes — the $10,000 income difference alone creates an entitlement to support.

Likely no — given the short marriage, absence of children, and near-equal incomes, there is no apparent compensatory basis (no career sacrifice or economic disadvantage from the marriage) and no strong non-compensatory basis (no significant need or dependency); a court would likely find no entitlement.
Yes — a spouse who earns less than the other is always entitled to receive spousal support.
expl: Entitlement to spousal support requires a basis in compensation, need/interdependency, or contract. A short marriage (4 years) with no children, equal careers, and near-equal incomes does not typically give rise to compensatory support (no career sacrifice or economic disadvantage caused by the marriage) or non-compensatory support (no significant need or dependency, given the high income). The income difference of $10,000 is not itself determinative of entitlement.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)

text:
Patricia and her husband Colin separated after 11 years of marriage with no dependent children. The court has already determined that Patricia is entitled to compensatory spousal support. Colin earns $130,000 per year and Patricia earns $52,000. Both lawyers refer to the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines to determine the appropriate amount and duration of support. Colin's lawyer argues the SSAG ranges are mandatory and the court must award support within the stated range.

What is the correct legal status of the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) in Ontario?
opts: The SSAG are binding on Ontario courts under the Divorce Act and the FLA; the court must stay within the calculated range.
The SSAG are not law; they are advisory only. Courts frequently consult them but are not required to follow them, and may depart from the SSAG range in appropriate circumstances.
The SSAG are binding only in the Superior Court of Justice, not on consent orders.
The SSAG replace the entitlement analysis; if the SSAG range produces a non-zero result, entitlement is automatically established.
expl: The Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines are advisory only. They were developed as a non-legislative tool to promote consistency and predictability in spousal support quantum and duration. Courts are not required to follow the SSAG but regularly consult them as a useful reference. Departure from the range is permitted where the circumstances of the case warrant a different result. The SSAG have not been enacted as legislation and have no binding statutory force.
caseRef: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, revised 2021); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Spousal Support | Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG)

text:
Kwame was injured in a car accident during the marriage. He received a damages award of $200,000, broken down as follows: $80,000 for pain and suffering, $70,000 for loss of future income, and $50,000 for future care costs. Kwame argues the entire $200,000 is excluded from his NFP as damages for personal injury.

What portion of Kwame's damages award, if any, is excluded from his net family property under FLA s. 4(2)?
