opts: All $200,000 is excluded — the entire damages award is for personal injury and therefore excluded.
Only the $80,000 for pain and suffering is excluded; the $70,000 for lost future income and the $50,000 for future care are included in NFP.
Nothing is excluded — damages awards of any kind are always included in NFP because they represent wealth accumulated during the marriage.
Only the $70,000 for lost future income is excluded because income is the primary purpose of FLA spousal protection.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(b), damages or a right to damages for personal injury are excluded from NFP, but only to the extent they represent compensation for non-pecuniary loss (pain and suffering, loss of amenities) and not for lost income or other income-replacement components. The $80,000 for pain and suffering qualifies as excluded property. The $70,000 for lost future income is a substitute for income that would have formed part of the marital economy and is included. The $50,000 for future care costs is similarly a pecuniary loss component not covered by the personal injury exclusion.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(b)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property Under FLA s. 4(2)

text:
Priya and her husband Rajan separate after 14 years of marriage. On the valuation date, Rajan's NFP is $410,000 and Priya's NFP is $170,000. Rajan argues that instead of paying Priya $120,000 in cash, the court should simply order him to transfer the couple's jointly held rental property worth $120,000 to Priya in satisfaction of the equalization payment. Priya opposes, wanting cash.

What is the correct characterization of the equalization payment and the court's power to satisfy it?
opts: The equalization payment automatically vests ownership of property in Priya; no court order is needed.

The equalization payment is a personal debt owed by Rajan to Priya; however, under FLA s. 9, the court has jurisdiction to order the transfer of specific property to satisfy that debt, even if Priya prefers cash.
Because Priya wants cash, the court has no power to order a property transfer in satisfaction of the equalization payment.
The equalization payment can only be satisfied by a cash payment; property transfers are not available under the FLA.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(1), the equalization payment is a debt owed by the higher-NFP spouse to the lower-NFP spouse. It does not automatically transfer title to any property. However, FLA s. 9(1) grants the court broad jurisdiction to order the transfer or partition of property, payment by installments, or other measures to satisfy the equalization payment. The court may therefore order a property transfer even if the recipient would prefer cash, where that is an appropriate remedy in the circumstances.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(1) and s. 9(1)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Calculating and Ordering the Equalization Payment

text:
The husband owned the matrimonial home before the marriage. On the date of marriage, the home was worth $200,000. On the valuation date, the home is worth $550,000. The husband seeks to deduct the $200,000 pre-marriage value from his NFP, arguing he brought that value into the marriage.

Can the husband deduct the $200,000 pre-marriage value of the matrimonial home from his net family property?
opts: Yes — the marriage-date deduction applies to all property including the matrimonial home.
Yes — he may deduct $200,000 because he owned the home before the marriage.

No — under FLA s.4(1)(b), the marriage-date deduction applies to all property 'other than a matrimonial home'; the matrimonial home cannot be deducted even if owned before marriage, so the full value of $550,000 is included in the husband's NFP without the marriage deduction.
Yes — he may deduct $200,000 because only the appreciation during the marriage should be shared.
expl: FLA s.4(1)(b) expressly carves out the matrimonial home from the marriage-date deduction. The formula reads: deduct the value of property owned at the date of marriage 'other than a matrimonial home.' This means that even if a spouse owned the matrimonial home before marriage, no deduction is available for its pre-marriage value. The entire $550,000 is included in the husband's NFP.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Matrimonial Home Exception to Marriage-Date Deduction

text:
The wife received spousal support for five years following separation. Her financial circumstances then improved significantly when she remarried a wealthy spouse, leaving her with no financial need. The husband brings an application to terminate the support order.

What legal principle governs whether a spousal support order may be varied or terminated?
opts: The husband may unilaterally stop making payments once the wife remarries, without a court order.
Spousal support cannot be varied once ordered unless both parties consent in writing.

A material change in circumstances since the original order was made allows either party to bring a variation application; the wife's remarriage and significant improvement in financial circumstances may constitute a material change justifying reduction or termination of support.
No variation is available during the first 10 years of a spousal support order.
expl: Under Divorce Act s.17 and FLA s.37, a court may vary or rescind a spousal support order where there has been a material change in circumstances since the order was made. The wife's remarriage and the elimination of her financial need are circumstances that a court would likely consider to constitute a material change, and the husband may succeed in obtaining a termination or reduction of the support order. Unilateral cessation of payments without a court order is not permitted.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 37; Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 17
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Variation of Spousal Support — Material Change in Circumstances

text:
During the marriage, Beatrice received $75,000 from her aunt's estate as a bequest. She kept the entire amount in a dedicated savings account in her own name, and no other funds were ever deposited into that account. On the valuation date, the account holds $75,000. Beatrice claims this inheritance is excluded from her NFP.

Is the $75,000 inheritance excluded from Beatrice's net family property?
opts: No — inheritances received during the marriage are always included in NFP because they represent wealth accumulated during the marriage.

Yes — FLA s. 4(2)(a) excludes property received by way of gift or inheritance from a third party received after the date of marriage, and the $75,000 is identifiable and traceable in a dedicated account.
No — only gifts from a spouse's parents are excluded; gifts from aunts are not covered by s. 4(2)(a).
Yes — but only if Beatrice can prove the inheritance was not used for family purposes during the marriage.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(a), property received by way of gift or inheritance from a third party after the date of marriage is excluded from the recipient spouse's net family property. The bequest from the aunt qualifies as an inheritance from a third party. Because Beatrice kept the funds in a dedicated account with no commingling, the property remains traceable and identifiable. The exclusion applies, and $75,000 is excluded from her NFP.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(a)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property Under FLA s. 4(2)

text:
On the valuation date, the wife's NFP calculates to negative $30,000 because her debts exceed her assets. The husband's NFP is $200,000. The parties dispute the equalization payment.

What equalization payment is payable and by whom?
opts: The wife pays the husband $15,000 (one-half of the negative NFP).
No equalization payment is payable because the wife has no positive NFP.
The husband pays the wife $100,000 — the wife's NFP is treated as zero under FLA s.4(5) because NFP cannot fall below zero; the equalization payment is ($200,000 − $0) ÷ 2 = $100,000.
The husband pays the wife $115,000 (one-half of $200,000 minus $30,000).
expl: FLA s.4(5) provides that if the application of the NFP formula would result in a negative amount, the amount is deemed to be zero. The wife's NFP is therefore $0, not −$30,000. The equalization payment under s.5(1) is one-half of the difference between the spouses' NFPs: ($200,000 − $0) ÷ 2 = $100,000, payable by the husband to the wife.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(5) and s. 5(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Equalization Payment and NFP Floor

text:
A court has found that the wife is entitled to spousal support. The husband argues that the finding of entitlement automatically determines both the amount and duration of support.

Does a finding of entitlement to spousal support automatically determine the amount and duration of the support award?
opts: Yes — once entitlement is established, the court must award the maximum amount specified by the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines.
Yes — a finding of entitlement automatically entitles the claimant to lifelong spousal support.

No — establishing entitlement is only the first step; amount and duration are then determined separately using factors under the FLA or Divorce Act, with the SSAG serving as a non-binding reference guide; the court may award a nominal amount or time-limited support even where entitlement exists.
Yes — once entitled, the court must award an amount equal to one-half the difference in the spouses' incomes.
expl: Spousal support analysis has three distinct stages: (1) entitlement, (2) amount, and (3) duration. A finding of entitlement does not predetermine the other two. The SSAG are non-binding guidelines that assist in calculating amount and duration but do not mandate any particular outcome. The court retains discretion and may award a nominal or time-limited amount even where entitlement is clear.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)

text:
Marcus and Ingrid separate. Marcus's NFP is $500,000 and Ingrid's NFP is $500,000. A friend tells Marcus that because their NFPs are equal, no equalization payment is owed, and Marcus does not need to consult a lawyer or do anything further with respect to the equalization claim. Ingrid has a different understanding.

When both spouses have equal NFPs, what equalization payment is owed?
opts: Each spouse pays the other $250,000 to reflect the shared marital wealth.
No equalization payment is owed — when both spouses have equal NFPs, the difference is zero and there is no obligation to pay under FLA s. 5(1); the friend's advice is correct on this point.
Ingrid pays Marcus $500,000 because she is the wife and traditionally receives property on marriage breakdown.
Marcus pays Ingrid $500,000 as the equalization payment equals each spouse's full NFP.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(1), the equalization payment equals one-half the difference between the higher and lower NFPs. Where both NFPs are equal — here both $500,000 — the difference is zero. One-half of zero is zero. No equalization payment is owed by either spouse. This is the correct legal result and the friend's advice on this specific point is accurate.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(1)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Calculating and Ordering the Equalization Payment

text:
The wife gave up her career as a licensed engineer to care for the couple's three children for 12 years. On separation, she has no income and limited prospects for re-entering the engineering profession at her former level. She seeks spousal support.

What ground of spousal support entitlement does this fact pattern most clearly engage?
opts: Contractual — the wife must show a written agreement obligating the husband to pay support.
Non-compensatory (need-based) — because the wife currently has no income.

Compensatory — the wife suffered economic disadvantage from the marriage and its breakdown by foregoing her engineering career to assume primary child care responsibilities, engaging FLA s.33(8)(a) and the Moge v Moge compensatory framework.
No entitlement — a spouse cannot claim support if she voluntarily left the workforce.
expl: A spouse who sacrificed a career to care for children during the marriage has suffered an economic disadvantage caused by the marriage and its breakdown. This is the paradigm case of compensatory support recognized in Moge v Moge, [1992] 3 SCR 813, and reflected in FLA s.33(8)(a) (spouse left employment to care for children or home) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)(a)(b). The primary basis here is compensatory, not need-based or contractual.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)

text:
The husband moved out of the matrimonial home following a dispute. While he was away, the wife changed the locks and refused to give him a key, preventing him from returning. The husband now wishes to re-enter the home.

What right does the husband have with respect to possession of the matrimonial home after voluntarily leaving?
opts: The wife may validly exclude the husband once she has changed the locks and physically asserted possession.
The husband has no right to re-enter the home once he has voluntarily left.

The husband retains his equal right to possession under FLA s.19 even after leaving; the wife cannot unilaterally exclude him; the husband may bring a motion to enforce his s.19 right, and the wife would need a court order under s.24 to validly bar his re-entry.
The husband must wait until the divorce proceedings are concluded before asserting his possession rights.
expl: FLA s.19(1) grants both spouses an equal right to possession throughout the marriage. Voluntarily leaving the matrimonial home does not waive or extinguish this right. The wife cannot unilaterally exclude the husband by changing the locks; she would need to obtain a court order for exclusive possession under s.24. The husband may bring a motion to enforce his s.19 rights.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19(1) and s. 24
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Equal Right of Possession — s. 19

text:
The husband's parents gave him $100,000 at the time of marriage. The gift deed expressly stated that any income earned on the gift was also to be excluded from the husband's NFP. During the marriage, the husband invested the $100,000 and it earned $40,000 in dividends. On the valuation date the account holds $165,000 (original $100,000 plus $40,000 dividends plus $25,000 in capital gains).

What amount may the husband exclude from his net family property?
opts: Nothing — gifts received at the time of marriage are not gifts received 'during' the marriage.
$100,000 only — income on excluded property is always included in NFP regardless of what the donor states.

$140,000 — the original $100,000 gift plus the $40,000 in dividends, because FLA s.4(2) provides that income on excluded property is also excluded where the donor or testator expressly provides it; the gift deed did so here.
$165,000 — the entire account is excluded because the initial contribution was a qualifying gift.
expl: FLA s.4(2) excludes gifts and inheritances received during the marriage, and its final paragraph provides that income from excluded property is also excluded 'if the donor or testator expressly provides.' The gift deed here expressly excluded income, so the $40,000 in dividends is excluded along with the $100,000 principal. The $25,000 in capital gains is not identified as 'income' in the same sense and would likely not qualify unless the deed also addressed capital appreciation; on exam facts as given, $140,000 is the excludable amount.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property — Income on Gifts

text:
The wife signed a separation agreement that expressly released all her rights under Part II of the FLA with respect to the matrimonial home. The husband later sold the home to a third party without obtaining any further consent from the wife. The wife now argues the sale should be set aside because she never consented to the actual sale.

Can the wife succeed in having the sale set aside?
opts: Yes — a separation agreement cannot override the requirements of s.21(1).
No — FLA s.21(1)(b) provides that no further consent is required where the non-owning spouse has released all Part II rights through a separation agreement.
Yes — the release in the separation agreement is only valid if the wife also received independent legal advice at the time she signed.
No — but only if the buyer was aware of the separation agreement.
expl: FLA s.21(1)(b) expressly provides that a spouse may dispose of the matrimonial home without the other's further consent if the other spouse 'has released all rights under this Part by a separation agreement.' The wife's release in the separation agreement satisfies this statutory exception; she cannot now seek to set aside the sale.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Release by Separation Agreement — s. 21(1)(b)

text:
Sylvie and her husband Bernard are separating. The matrimonial home is registered in Bernard's name only. Sylvie has two children, ages 7 and 10, who are enrolled in school nearby. Bernard insists that because he owns the home, Sylvie and the children must leave immediately. Sylvie brings a motion for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home.

Which statement correctly describes the court's power to grant exclusive possession under FLA s. 24(1)?
opts: The court cannot grant exclusive possession to Sylvie because Bernard is the registered owner of the home.
The court may grant exclusive possession of the matrimonial home to Sylvie for a period of time under FLA s. 24(1), regardless of which spouse owns the home.
The court may grant exclusive possession only if Bernard has committed an act of violence against Sylvie.
Exclusive possession can only be granted as part of a final divorce order, not on an interim motion.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1), the court may order exclusive possession of the matrimonial home or any part of it to one spouse for a specified period, regardless of ownership. The non-owner spouse can receive an exclusive possession order even where the other spouse holds legal title. Ownership is one consideration, but it is not a bar to granting exclusive possession to the non-owning spouse.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Matrimonial Home | Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24

text:
On the date of marriage, the wife owned a car worth $18,000 and RRSP savings of $32,000, with no debts. By the valuation date, the car is worth $0 (it was scrapped years ago) and the RRSP has grown to $75,000. The wife claims a marriage-date deduction.

What is the wife's marriage-date deduction under FLA s.4(1)(b)?
opts: $32,000 — only the RRSP value at marriage, since the car no longer exists.
$75,000 — the current value of the RRSP.
$50,000 — the total value of property owned at the date of marriage ($18,000 car + $32,000 RRSP), regardless of whether those assets still exist on the valuation date.
$0 — because the car no longer exists, no deduction is available.
expl: FLA s.4(1)(b) deducts the value of property other than the matrimonial home that was owned on the date of marriage, calculated at its marriage-date value. Neither the continued existence of the asset nor its current value is relevant. The wife deducts $18,000 (car) + $32,000 (RRSP) = $50,000.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Property Owned at Date of Marriage

text:
On the valuation date, a husband has total assets of $300,000 and debts of $50,000. On the date of marriage he owned property worth $80,000 (not the matrimonial home) and had no debts at that time.

What is the husband's net family property?
opts: The husband's NFP is $300,000
The husband's NFP is $250,000
The husband's NFP is $170,000
The husband's NFP is $220,000
expl: Under FLA s.4(1), net family property = (assets on valuation date) − (debts on valuation date) − (value of property other than the matrimonial home owned on the date of marriage, minus debts at marriage). Here: $300,000 − $50,000 − ($80,000 − $0) = $170,000.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | The NFP Formula

text:
During the marriage, the husband received a gift of $50,000 from his parents. He used the entire gift to pay down the mortgage on the matrimonial home. On the valuation date, he claims $50,000 as excluded property under FLA s.4(2)(c).

Can the husband exclude the $50,000 gift from his net family property?
opts: Yes — all gifts received during the marriage are excluded under s.4(2)(c) regardless of how they are used.
Yes — but only if the husband can prove the gift was received from his parents.

No — once the gift was used to pay down the mortgage on the matrimonial home (which itself cannot be deducted), the gift value is absorbed into the home's equity and cannot be separately excluded; the home's full value enters NFP without deduction.
Yes — but only the original $50,000; any appreciation in the home attributable to the gift is included in NFP.
expl: FLA s.4(2)(c) excludes property received by gift during the marriage, but the exclusion applies to the property itself in its traceable form. When the gift money was used to pay down the mortgage on the matrimonial home, it became equity in the home. The matrimonial home cannot receive a marriage-date deduction (s.4(1)(b)), and its full value enters NFP. The gift, once absorbed into the home's equity, loses its identifiable excluded character and cannot be separately carved out.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(c) and s. 4(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property — Gifts and Matrimonial Home Interaction

text:
Before the marriage, Nadia's grandmother left her a cottage in her will. After the marriage, Nadia and her husband Luca moved into the cottage and used it as their primary family home for the entire 10-year marriage. On the valuation date, the cottage is worth $620,000. Nadia claims the cottage is excluded from her NFP because she received it as an inheritance.

Is the cottage excluded from Nadia's net family property?
opts: Yes — the cottage was inherited before the marriage, so it qualifies as property owned at the date of marriage and receives a marriage-date deduction equal to its value at that time; no exclusion analysis is needed.
No — property inherited before the marriage is included in NFP with no deduction because it pre-dates the marriage.

No — even if the cottage would otherwise qualify as excluded property under s. 4(2)(a) as an inheritance, the FLA provides that the matrimonial home cannot be excluded from NFP; because the cottage became the matrimonial home during the marriage, it is not excluded and its full value is included in Nadia's NFP.
Yes — the cottage is excluded as an inheritance regardless of whether it later became the matrimonial home, because the exclusion attached at the time it was received.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2), the general rule excludes property received by gift or inheritance after the date of marriage. However, s. 4(2) contains a proviso that the matrimonial home is not excluded from NFP even if received as a gift or inheritance. The cottage, though inherited, was used as the couple's primary family home throughout the marriage, making it the matrimonial home. The matrimonial home exception to the exclusion rule applies, and the full value of $620,000 enters Nadia's NFP without exclusion. Note also that because it was inherited before the marriage, it is treated as property owned at the date of marriage (not excluded under s. 4(2)(a), which applies to post-marriage receipts); instead, a marriage-date deduction under s. 4(1)(b) would be unavailable because it is the matrimonial home.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(a) and s. 4(1)(b)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property Under FLA s. 4(2)

text:
Obiageli brings a motion for exclusive possession of the matrimonial home. Both she and her husband Emeka are registered owners. The couple's three children, aged 5, 9, and 13, live in the home and attend schools within walking distance. Emeka earns $180,000 per year and can afford alternative accommodation. Obiageli earns $42,000 per year. The court must apply FLA s. 24(3) to decide the motion.

Which factors under FLA s. 24(3) are most directly engaged in Obiageli's favour on these facts?
opts: The fact that Obiageli is a joint owner of the home is the determinative factor in her favour.

The best interests of the three children and the significant disparity in the parties' financial positions and ability to secure alternative accommodation are the most directly engaged s. 24(3) factors supporting Obiageli's motion.
Obiageli's motion will succeed only if she can prove that Emeka has committed family violence.
The only relevant factor is the length of the marriage; exclusive possession is routinely granted in long marriages.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3), the court must consider: the best interests of the children, the financial situation of both spouses, the availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and any other relevant circumstances. On these facts, the best interests of three school-age children (proximity to schools, stability) and the parties' divergent financial capacity to secure alternative accommodation (Emeka at $180,000 vs. Obiageli at $42,000) are the most directly engaged factors. Joint ownership is relevant but not determinative.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Matrimonial Home | Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24

text:
A husband and wife are calculating their respective NFPs on separation. Both owned property at the date of marriage. The husband asks why the FLA allows a deduction for property owned at the date of marriage.

What is the purpose of the marriage-date deduction in the NFP formula?
opts: To penalize spouses who entered the marriage with greater wealth.
To ensure that only wealth accumulated during the marriage is subject to equalization on breakdown, not pre-marital assets.
To ensure both spouses have equal NFP at the end of the marriage.
To prevent the non-owning spouse from making any claim against property owned before the marriage date.
expl: The marriage-date deduction reflects the legislative intent that equalization should share only the wealth accumulated during the marriage through the combined efforts and shared life of the spouses. Property owned before the marriage represents the individual wealth each spouse brought in, which is not intended to be shared through the equalization regime.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Property Owned at Date of Marriage

text:
A court is hearing a motion for an exclusive possession order in favour of the wife and the couple's two young children while the divorce proceedings are pending. The husband opposes, arguing the order cannot be made because he is the sole owner of the home.

What test does FLA s.24 apply when determining whether to grant an exclusive possession order?
opts: Ownership of the home is the primary factor in awarding exclusive possession.
The court must grant exclusive possession if one spouse has left the home.

Under FLA s.24, the court considers: the best interests of the children, each spouse's financial position, the availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and other relevant circumstances; ownership of the property is not determinative.
Exclusive possession can only be granted as part of a final divorce order.
expl: FLA s.24(3) sets out the factors the court must consider on a motion for exclusive possession: the best interests of the children, the financial position of both spouses, availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and any other relevant circumstances. Ownership is not determinative; a non-owning spouse may receive an exclusive possession order where the circumstances warrant it.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Exclusive Possession — s. 24 Test

text:
Yusuf's NFP on the valuation date is $900,000. His wife Farida's NFP is $100,000. During the marriage Yusuf gambled recklessly over three years, losing $400,000 of what would otherwise have been family wealth. Farida applies for an increased equalization payment under FLA s. 5(6), arguing the standard equalization payment of $400,000 would be unconscionable in light of Yusuf's gambling. Yusuf argues the court has no power to increase the equalization payment.

Does the court have jurisdiction to increase the equalization payment beyond the standard s. 5(1) amount?
opts: No — the equalization payment under s. 5(1) is fixed by statute and cannot be altered by the court under any circumstances.
No — s. 5(6) only permits the court to decrease the equalization payment, never to increase it.

Yes — FLA s. 5(6) gives the court jurisdiction to award an amount that is more or less than the s. 5(1) equalization payment where the standard result would be unconscionable, and reckless dissipation of assets is an express s. 5(6) factor.
Yes — but only if both parties consent to the court exercising the s. 5(6) jurisdiction.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(6), the court may award an amount that is more or less than the equalization payment calculated under s. 5(1) if the court is of the opinion that equalizing the NFPs would be unconscionable, having regard to certain factors. One express factor is a spouse's reckless or negligent depletion of their net family property (s. 5(6)(h)). Yusuf's reckless gambling losses are directly relevant. The court may therefore increase the payment to Farida beyond $400,000.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(6)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Calculating and Ordering the Equalization Payment

text:
Husband and wife separate. The wife vacates the matrimonial home. The husband, as sole owner, assumes that now that they are separated and the wife has moved out, he may sell the home without her consent. He proceeds to list and sell the home without obtaining the wife's consent.

Does separation and the wife's departure from the matrimonial home extinguish her s.21 consent rights?
opts: Yes — once spouses separate, the non-owning spouse loses all s.21 rights.
Yes — once the wife has vacated the home, she has abandoned her Part II rights.

No — FLA s.21 continues to apply after separation; the wife's consent is still required unless she has released her rights through a domestic contract, a court order has been obtained, or she has joined in the instrument.
Yes — separation extinguishes s.21 rights, but the husband must still obtain a court order confirming this.
expl: FLA s.21(1) does not contain a separation exception. The consent requirement continues to apply after separation until one of the three exceptions is met: (a) the other spouse joins in the instrument or consents in writing, (b) the other spouse has released all Part II rights through a separation agreement or other domestic contract, or (c) a court order authorizes the disposition. Physical departure from the home does not constitute an abandonment of Part II rights.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Consent Required to Dispose — s. 21(1)

text:
During the marriage, the husband is the sole registered owner of the couple's family home. The wife has never been on title. She asks her lawyer whether she has any right to remain in the home.

What right does FLA s.19 give the wife with respect to the matrimonial home?
opts: The wife has no right to remain because she does not own the home.
Both spouses have an equal right to possession of the matrimonial home regardless of which spouse owns it, under FLA s.19(1).
The wife has a right to possession only if she has contributed financially to the mortgage payments.
The wife's right to possession depends entirely on a court order.
expl: FLA s.19(1) grants both spouses an equal right to possession of every property that is their matrimonial home. This right exists independently of ownership; legal title in one spouse does not deprive the other of possession rights. The right arises by statute, not by court order.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Equal Right of Possession — s. 19

text:
After separation, the husband moves out of the matrimonial home but continues to pay the mortgage. He later argues he is entitled to exclusive possession of the home because he is the sole owner and the sole financial supporter of the property.

Is the husband automatically entitled to exclusive possession of the matrimonial home because he is the owner and pays the mortgage?
opts: Yes — as owner and financial supporter, the husband is automatically entitled to exclusive possession.
Yes — once a spouse voluntarily leaves the matrimonial home, the remaining spouse automatically has exclusive possession.

No — both spouses retain their equal right of possession under s.19(1) regardless of ownership; a spouse seeking exclusive possession must obtain a court order under FLA s.24, which the court grants only where it considers it appropriate having regard to the best interests of any child or the personal protection of a spouse.
Yes — once one spouse has moved out, the remaining spouse obtains exclusive possession by default.
expl: Ownership and financial contribution do not automatically confer exclusive possession under the FLA. FLA s.19(1) preserves equal possession rights for both spouses. To obtain exclusive possession, the husband would need a court order under s.24, where the court weighs statutory factors including the best interests of any children, financial position of each spouse, availability of other accommodation, and any violence.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 19(1) and s. 24
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Equal Right of Possession — s. 19

text:
During the marriage, the wife provided written consent to the sale of the matrimonial home. The agreement of purchase and sale was signed by the husband and the buyer in reliance on the wife's consent. Six months later, before the closing date, the wife purports to revoke her consent.

Can the wife effectively revoke her consent to the sale of the matrimonial home after the agreement of purchase and sale has been entered into in reliance on it?
opts: Yes — consent can always be revoked before the transaction closes.
No — consent given under s.21 can never be revoked under any circumstances.

No — once a spouse has consented and the other party has relied on that consent by entering into a binding agreement of purchase and sale, the consent generally cannot be revoked; revocation may only be possible before any reliance occurs.
Yes — the wife may revoke consent at any time before the transfer is registered on title.
expl: Once a binding agreement of purchase and sale has been entered into in reliance on the consenting spouse's consent, revocation is generally not effective to defeat the transaction. The consent was given to enable the very transaction that is now binding on the parties. Revocation before any party has relied on the consent might be possible, but once the transaction is made, the consent cannot be withdrawn to frustrate the sale. The purchaser's rights are also engaged.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Revocation of Consent — s. 21

text:
A couple owns both a primary residence in the city and a cottage at which they spend summers. They ordinarily occupy both properties as their family residence throughout the year. Both properties are sold after separation. The wife challenges whether FLA s.19 applied to both.

Can both the city home and the cottage qualify as matrimonial homes to which FLA s.19 applies?
opts: Only the primary residence is a matrimonial home; a cottage cannot qualify.
A property used only seasonally cannot be a matrimonial home.
Both properties can qualify as matrimonial homes under FLA s.18(2) if both were ordinarily occupied by both spouses as their family residence; FLA s.19 rights apply to each qualifying property.
The couple must have formally designated one property as the matrimonial home for s.19 to apply.
expl: FLA s.18(1) defines a matrimonial home as every property in which a person has an interest and that is or was at the time of separation ordinarily occupied by the person and his or her spouse as their family residence. There is no limit to one matrimonial home per couple (s.18(2) permits multiple). Seasonal or summer use can satisfy 'ordinarily occupied' if the family regularly used the property as a family residence. Both properties can be matrimonial homes and s.19 applies to each.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 18(1), s. 18(2), and s. 19
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Multiple Matrimonial Homes — s. 18(2)

text:
Husband and wife both owned property at the date of marriage: the husband owned a portfolio worth $120,000 and the wife owned a car and savings totalling $45,000. Neither held the matrimonial home at marriage. They separate 12 years later and both claim the marriage-date deduction.

Is the marriage-date deduction available to both spouses?
opts: No — only the higher-earning spouse is entitled to the marriage-date deduction.
No — the marriage-date deduction is available only to the spouse who initiates the equalization claim.
Yes — each spouse independently calculates their own NFP; each is entitled to deduct from their own NFP the value of non-matrimonial-home property they owned at the date of marriage.
No — the deduction is shared between the spouses and cannot be claimed by both simultaneously.
expl: FLA s.4(1) requires each spouse to calculate their own NFP independently using the formula. The marriage-date deduction is applied by each spouse against their own pre-marital assets. There is no rule limiting the deduction to only one spouse, to the higher earner, or to the party initiating the claim. Each spouse deducts their own marriage-date property from their own NFP.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(1)(b)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Property Owned at Date of Marriage

text:
During the marriage, the husband received an inheritance of $150,000. He deposited the entire amount into a joint bank account he shared with his wife, where it commingled with other family funds. On the valuation date, $80,000 remains in the account. The husband argues the inheritance is excluded property under FLA s.4(2)(c).

Is the inheritance excluded from the husband's net family property?
opts: Yes — all inheritances received during the marriage are automatically excluded from NFP under s.4(2)(c) regardless of how they are held.
No — inheritances are never excluded from NFP.

Likely no — FLA s.4(2)(c) excludes property received by inheritance during the marriage, but only if it remains traceable; depositing the inheritance into a joint account with the spouse likely destroys the exclusion by commingling it with other assets.
Yes — but only the original $150,000; growth or income earned on excluded property is included in NFP.
expl: FLA s.4(2)(c) excludes property received by gift or inheritance during the marriage, but the exclusion requires the property to be traceable. When excluded property is deposited into a joint account and commingled with other funds, traceability is typically lost, defeating the exclusion. The commingling issue must be resolved before any growth/income analysis under the last paragraph of s.4(2) becomes relevant.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(2)(c)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | Excluded Property and Traceability

text:
The court grants Théodore's wife Monique an exclusive possession order over the matrimonial home for 18 months, during which Théodore is prohibited from entering or occupying the property. Théodore's lawyer later tells him that the exclusive possession order has transferred legal title to the home to Monique and that he is no longer an owner. Théodore is alarmed by this advice.

What is the legal effect of an exclusive possession order under FLA s. 24(1) on the ownership of the matrimonial home?
opts: An exclusive possession order transfers legal title to the matrimonial home to the spouse who receives the order.
An exclusive possession order is a temporary right of occupation only; it does not transfer ownership or title to the home; Théodore remains a legal owner despite the order.
An exclusive possession order operates as a constructive trust, entitling Monique to a beneficial interest in the home equal to the period of exclusive occupation.
An exclusive possession order automatically converts the matrimonial home into jointly held property upon its expiry.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1), an exclusive possession order grants the right to reside in and occupy the matrimonial home to one spouse for a specified period. It is a personal right of occupation, not a transfer of ownership. Legal title remains unchanged; Théodore remains a registered owner throughout the order. The order bars him from entering or occupying the home during the specified period but does not divest him of his ownership interest.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(1)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Matrimonial Home | Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24

text:
At the date of marriage, the wife had savings of $40,000 and no debts. She spent the savings during the marriage on a vacation. On the valuation date her total assets are $200,000 and her debts are $30,000. The husband challenges whether the wife may still deduct the $40,000.

Is the wife entitled to deduct the $40,000 marriage-date savings from her net family property even though the savings no longer exist?
opts: No — the deduction is only available if the property still exists on the valuation date.
No — she voluntarily dissipated the assets, forfeiting the deduction.
Yes — FLA s.4(1)(b) deducts the value of property owned at the date of marriage regardless of whether that property still exists on the valuation date; the wife may deduct $40,000.
Yes — but only if she can prove she would have retained the savings absent the marriage.
expl: The marriage-date deduction under FLA s.4(1)(b) is fixed at the value of property owned on the date of marriage. The FLA does not require the property to still exist at the valuation date, and there is no dissipation rule that forfeits the deduction. The wife may therefore deduct $40,000 in calculating her NFP.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 4(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Net Family Property and Equalization | The NFP Formula

text:
Anita and Darnell separate. There have been no incidents of family violence. Anita's lawyer tells her that she cannot obtain an exclusive possession order because there has been no violence and the children, aged 16 and 17, are almost adults. Darnell's lawyer tells him that an exclusive possession order will certainly be granted because Anita is the lower-income spouse. Both lawyers assert their positions as categorical rules.

Which statement most accurately reflects the law on exclusive possession under FLA s. 24?
opts: Anita's lawyer is correct: an exclusive possession order cannot be made without evidence of family violence.
Darnell's lawyer is correct: lower income alone entitles a spouse to exclusive possession.

Neither lawyer is correct; under FLA s. 24(3) the court considers multiple factors including but not limited to violence, financial position, availability of other accommodation, and best interests of children; no single factor is conclusive, and the absence of violence does not bar an order, nor does income disparity alone guarantee one.
Exclusive possession cannot be ordered where the children are teenagers because their best interests are less weighty than those of younger children.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3), the court must weigh all relevant circumstances, including the best interests of any children regardless of age, the financial situation of both spouses, the availability of other appropriate accommodation, any violence or abuse, and other relevant factors. No single factor is determinative. The absence of violence does not categorically bar an exclusive possession order — other factors may support it. Equally, financial disparity alone does not guarantee an order; the court exercises discretion based on the full picture.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 24(3)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Matrimonial Home | Exclusive Possession Orders — s. 24

text:
The husband is the sole registered owner of the matrimonial home. Without telling his wife, he signs an agreement of purchase and sale to sell the home to a buyer. The wife has not consented to the sale.

What does FLA s.21(1) require before the husband may validly sell the matrimonial home?
opts: The husband may sell his own property without restriction since he is the sole registered owner.

The husband must obtain the wife's written consent or joinder in the instrument, a court order authorizing the sale, or a prior release of the wife's Part II rights; failing any of these, the sale may be set aside.
The husband must notify the wife of the sale; her consent is not required.
The s.21(1) restriction applies only if the wife's name appears on title.
expl: FLA s.21(1) provides that a spouse shall not dispose of or encumber an interest in the matrimonial home unless the other spouse joins in the instrument or consents in writing, or has released all rights under Part II by separation agreement, or a court order authorizes the disposition. Sole ownership of the matrimonial home does not exempt a spouse from this requirement.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(1)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Consent Required to Dispose — s. 21(1)

text:
The husband sold the matrimonial home to a purchaser who had no knowledge that the property was the couple's matrimonial home. The wife had not consented to the sale. The wife seeks to have the transaction set aside under FLA s.21(3).

What effect does the purchaser's lack of notice of the matrimonial home status have on the wife's s.21(3) application?
opts: The sale can always be set aside where the non-consenting spouse did not consent, regardless of what the purchaser knew.

The purchaser is protected; under FLA s.21(3), where a spouse disposes of the matrimonial home without the other's consent and the purchaser acquired for value without notice that the property was a matrimonial home, the court will not set aside the transaction.
The wife can set aside the sale regardless of what the purchaser knew, because s.21 consent is a prerequisite to a valid disposition.
The purchaser must demonstrate that they conducted a registry search before the court will protect the transaction.
expl: FLA s.21(3) provides that where a spouse purports to dispose of the matrimonial home without the required consent, the court may set aside the transaction unless the purchaser acquired the interest for value without notice that the property was a matrimonial home at the time of the disposition. The bona fide purchaser for value without notice is protected, and the wife's application will fail.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 21(3)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 4 — Matrimonial Home | Purchaser for Value Without Notice — s. 21(3)

text:
Husband and wife both worked throughout their 20-year marriage and maintained equal incomes. Near the end of the marriage, the wife developed a serious illness that left her permanently unable to work. The illness was not caused by or related to the marriage. On separation, the wife seeks spousal support.

What ground of spousal support entitlement most appropriately applies?
opts: Compensatory — because the marriage caused the illness.
No entitlement — a spouse cannot claim support for a medical condition unrelated to the marriage.

Non-compensatory — under Bracklow v Bracklow, 1999 SCC, support may be awarded on a non-compensatory basis where the spouse has a need arising from the marriage and its breakdown, grounded in the relationship of mutual interdependency, even where the marriage did not cause the economic disadvantage.
Contractual — the wife needs a pre-existing support agreement to establish entitlement.
expl: Where there is no economic disadvantage caused by the marriage (the illness was not marriage-related), compensatory support does not apply. Bracklow v Bracklow, [1999] 1 SCR 420 recognized that support may also be awarded on a non-compensatory basis, reflecting the social obligation and mutual interdependency arising from marriage. The wife's need resulting from a disability arising near the end of a long marriage may engage this ground.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 33(8); Divorce Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. 3 (2nd Supp.), s. 15.2(6)
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 5 — Spousal Support | Entitlement Grounds — FLA s.33(8) and Divorce Act s.15.2(6)

text:
On the valuation date, Dimitri's NFP is $320,000 and his wife Sonja's NFP is $80,000. Neither spouse has any excluded property or debt at marriage. They agree on the NFP figures but dispute whether an equalization payment is owed and by whom.

What equalization payment is payable, and who pays whom?
opts: Sonja pays Dimitri $80,000 because she has the lower NFP and must contribute to equalization.
Dimitri pays Sonja $120,000, representing one-half of the difference between their net family properties under FLA s. 5(1).
No equalization payment is owed because equalization simply divides all property equally between the spouses.
Dimitri pays Sonja $160,000, representing the full difference between their net family properties.
expl: Under the Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(1), the spouse with the lower NFP is entitled to one-half the difference between the spouses' net family properties. Here: (Higher NFP − Lower NFP) ÷ 2 = ($320,000 − $80,000) ÷ 2 = $240,000 ÷ 2 = $120,000. Dimitri, having the higher NFP, pays Sonja $120,000.
caseRef: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 5(1)
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Net Family Property and Equalization | Calculating and Ordering the Equalization Payment

text:
A government agency sends a letter to an applicant for a business permit stating that it will provide her with an opportunity to make submissions before any final decision is made. Relying on this letter, the applicant prepares her case. The agency then makes a final decision adverse to her without providing any opportunity to be heard.

Which Baker factor is most directly engaged by the agency's conduct, and what is the legal consequence?
opts: Factor 3 — the decision must have been important to the applicant, entitling her to a hearing.
Factor 2 — the statute governing the agency must have required consultation before a final decision.

Factor 4 — legitimate expectations — the agency's representation that it would provide an opportunity for submissions created a procedural legitimate expectation; proceeding without that opportunity constituted a breach of the duty of fairness.
No Baker factor is engaged because government representations do not create enforceable procedural rights.
expl: Baker Factor 4 addresses legitimate expectations: where a public authority has represented, through its conduct or promises, that a particular procedure will be followed, a procedural legitimate expectation arises. The applicant is then entitled to that procedure before an adverse decision is made. The agency's failure to honour its promise breached the duty of fairness.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | The Duty of Fairness and Baker Factors

text:
An Ontario tribunal governed by the Statutory Powers Procedure Act holds a hearing on a complaint against a licensed contractor. The contractor is ordered to repay $15,000 in fees to a consumer. The contractor's representative requests written reasons for the decision within 25 days of receiving the decision. The tribunal refuses to provide reasons, stating that the SPPA does not always require them.

Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, is the tribunal required to provide written reasons in these circumstances?
opts: No. The SPPA never requires written reasons unless the tribunal's enabling statute specifically mandates them.
Yes. The SPPA requires written reasons for every administrative decision, regardless of whether a hearing was held.

Yes. Under s. 17 of the SPPA, a tribunal that holds a hearing must give written reasons for its final decision if a party requests them; the contractor's request within 25 days satisfies the timing requirement.
No. Written reasons are only required under the SPPA where the decision results in imprisonment or loss of a professional licence.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17, a tribunal that has held a hearing must give written reasons for its decision to any party who requests them. The request must be made within the time prescribed by the tribunal's rules, or within 30 days of receiving the decision if no time is prescribed. The contractor's request within 25 days satisfies this requirement, and the tribunal must provide written reasons.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17; Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Administrative Law — Principles and Natural Justice | Right to Reasons for Administrative Decisions

text:
An Ontario College of Physicians and Surgeons panel revokes a physician's certificate of registration for conduct unbecoming a member of the profession. The physician's enabling statute contains a provision allowing any party to appeal the College's decision to the Divisional Court on any question of law. The physician argues on appeal that the College misinterpreted the statutory definition of 'conduct unbecoming.' The College argues reasonableness applies because it has expertise in professional standards.

What standard of review applies to the College's interpretation of 'conduct unbecoming' given the statutory right of appeal on questions of law?
opts: Reasonableness, because professional regulatory bodies always attract deference on matters involving professional standards.
Reasonableness, because the definition of 'conduct unbecoming' is a question of mixed fact and law to which expertise-based deference applies.

Correctness, because under Vavilov the presence of a statutory right of appeal on a question of law signals legislative intent that the appellate court is to apply the standard applicable in that context — which for questions of law is correctness.
Correctness, because licence revocation is a decision that attracts the highest level of scrutiny regardless of the appeal mechanism.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the Supreme Court of Canada held that where legislation provides a statutory right of appeal on a question of law, this signals that the legislature intended the appellate court to apply the appellate standards, which for questions of law is correctness. The statutory right of appeal rebuts the presumption of reasonableness. Because the physician is appealing on a question of law — the interpretation of 'conduct unbecoming' — the Divisional Court applies correctness and substitutes its own interpretation if necessary.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Correctness Standard of Review

text:
An Ontario tribunal governed by the SPPA holds a two-day hearing on a regulatory matter. Its enabling statute prescribes a 21-day window within which a party may request written reasons. A party requests reasons on day 25, after the 21-day window has closed, believing the general 30-day SPPA default applies. The tribunal refuses to provide written reasons.

Is the tribunal's refusal to provide written reasons lawful where the request was made after the enabling statute's prescribed time limit but within the general 30-day SPPA default?
opts: No. The SPPA's 30-day default window always governs and overrides any shorter period prescribed by the enabling statute.
No. Tribunals have a common law duty to give reasons whenever a hearing is held, which cannot be limited by statute.

Yes. Under SPPA s. 17, the time for requesting written reasons is the period prescribed by the tribunal's rules or enabling statute; where the enabling statute prescribes 21 days and the request was made on day 25, the party has missed the statutory deadline and the tribunal's refusal is lawful.
Yes, but only if the tribunal can demonstrate it was prejudiced by the late request.
expl: Under the Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17, a party may request written reasons within the time prescribed by the tribunal's rules, or within 30 days after receiving the decision if no time is prescribed. The 30-day default applies only where the enabling statute or tribunal rules do not themselves prescribe a time limit. Here, the enabling statute prescribes 21 days, which governs; the general 30-day SPPA default does not override a specific statutory prescription. The request on day 25 was late, and the tribunal's refusal is lawful.
caseRef: Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Administrative Law — Principles and Natural Justice | Right to Reasons for Administrative Decisions

text:
The College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario imposes restrictions on a physician's hospital privileges following a quality assurance review. In its written reasons, the College applies a legal test for 'fitness to practise' that it developed internally. The physician argues on judicial review that the College applied an incorrect legal test and that the correct test should be narrower. The reviewing court agrees the test is debatable but concludes it falls within a range of defensible interpretations of the applicable legislation.

On reasonableness review, what is the court's role when a tribunal applies a legal test drawn from its home statute that the court believes may not be the best interpretation?
opts: The court must substitute its own preferred interpretation of the legal test whenever it disagrees with the tribunal's approach.
The court will uphold the tribunal's legal test without any analysis, as questions of law within a tribunal's home statute are immune from judicial review.

Under Vavilov, on reasonableness review the court does not ask whether the tribunal adopted the correct legal test in the court's view; rather, it asks whether the test applied falls within a range of possible acceptable outcomes defensible in law; if so, the decision is reasonable even if the court would have chosen differently.
The court must apply correctness review to any legal test a tribunal derives from its home statute, because legal interpretation is a judicial function.
expl: Under Dunsmuir v New Brunswick, 2008 SCC 9, and as refined by Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, reasonableness review asks whether the decision falls within a range of possible acceptable outcomes that are defensible in respect of the facts and law. The court does not substitute its own preferred interpretation; the existence of multiple defensible interpretations of a statute is precisely the situation where deference to the tribunal's expertise is appropriate, provided the interpretation can be justified with reference to the text, purpose, and context of the statute.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Reasonableness Standard of Review

text:
A provincial law requires applicants for a certain government administrative position to be Canadian citizens. A permanent resident who is otherwise qualified is denied the position solely because of their immigration status. They challenge the law under s. 15 of the Charter.

Is citizenship an enumerated or analogous ground for the purposes of s. 15 of the Charter?
opts: No. Only the grounds explicitly listed in s. 15(1) qualify; citizenship is not among them.
Yes. Citizenship is an analogous ground established in Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia; a distinction based on citizenship can engage s. 15(1).
Citizenship is an analogous ground only when it overlaps with national or ethnic origin.
The law is automatically constitutional because citizenship is a matter of federal jurisdiction.
expl: In Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia, [1989] 1 SCR 143, the Supreme Court of Canada recognized citizenship as an analogous ground under s. 15. Analogous grounds are personal characteristics that are immutable or changeable only at unacceptable personal cost, rendering the group politically powerless.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia, [1989] 1 SCR 143
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 15 — Equality Rights and the Andrews Test

text:
A social assistance review tribunal makes a decision that an applicant submits exceeds its statutory jurisdiction. The applicant argues that correctness review must apply because any decision made in excess of jurisdiction is, by definition, a jurisdictional question attracting correctness. The tribunal argues all its decisions — including jurisdictional determinations — are reviewable only on a reasonableness standard.

Under Vavilov, does correctness apply to all decisions a tribunal makes that could be characterized as 'jurisdictional' in nature?
opts: Yes. Any decision a tribunal makes that can be described as going to its jurisdiction — including exceeding its statutory mandate — attracts correctness review under Vavilov.
No. Vavilov eliminated the old jurisdictional correctness category entirely; all administrative decisions now attract reasonableness without exception.

No. Vavilov significantly narrowed the jurisdictional correctness category; correctness for jurisdictional reasons now applies only where there is a question of jurisdiction between two or more administrative bodies, not simply where a tribunal may have exceeded its statutory mandate.
Yes. The statutory mandate of a tribunal is always a jurisdictional question, and courts apply correctness whenever a tribunal may have acted beyond its enabling statute.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the Supreme Court of Canada explicitly narrowed the jurisdictional correctness category. Post-Vavilov, 'jurisdictional' correctness applies only to disputes about jurisdiction between two or more administrative bodies — for example, where two tribunals each claim or disclaim authority over the same matter. A tribunal allegedly exceeding its statutory mandate is not treated as a jurisdictional question attracting correctness; instead, whether a tribunal acted within its enabling statute is reviewed on a reasonableness standard as a question of the tribunal's interpretation of its own home statute.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Correctness Standard of Review

text:
After a full Charter challenge, a court finds that a provincial law restricting Sunday business hours infringes freedom of religion but that the government has satisfied every element of the Oakes test: the objective is pressing and substantial, and the measures are rationally connected, minimally impairing, and proportionate in their effects.

What is the legal effect when a government successfully satisfies all elements of the Oakes test under s. 1 of the Charter?
opts: The law is struck down and must be redrafted to fully eliminate the Charter infringement.
The court must refer the matter to the legislature for review before the law can be upheld.
The Charter infringement is justified under s. 1; the law is saved and upheld as constitutional.
The court upholds the law but must order the government to compensate those whose rights were limited.
expl: When a court finds a Charter infringement but the government satisfies all elements of the Oakes test, the limitation is 'demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society' under s. 1. The law is upheld as constitutional and no remedy is granted.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | The Oakes Test — Two-Stage Analysis

text:
A federal statute makes it an offence for anyone to assist a person in living near a drug injection site, regardless of whether the person assisted is actually a drug user. The government argues the law is not overbroad because it does catch some drug dealers and genuine enablers of addiction. The claimants argue the law captures innocent bystanders and family members.

What is the correct overbreadth analysis under s. 7 of the Charter as articulated in R v Bedford?
opts: A law is overbroad under s. 7 only if it has absolutely no application to legitimate targets.
A law is overbroad only if the legislature subjectively intended to capture more people than the objective requires.

Under R v Bedford, a law is overbroad if it is broader than necessary to achieve the state's objective; even if the law applies to some legitimate targets, it may be overbroad where it captures substantial amounts of innocent conduct not connected to the legislative purpose.
A law is overbroad under s. 7 only if it simultaneously violates another Charter right.
expl: R v Bedford, 2013 SCC 72, established that overbreadth examines whether the law captures conduct or persons beyond what is necessary to achieve the legislative purpose. The existence of some legitimate targets does not save a law from overbreadth if it also captures substantial amounts of innocent conduct unconnected to its objective.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 7 — Life, Liberty, and Security of the Person

text:
A provincial law imposing a comprehensive ban on tobacco advertising is challenged under the Charter. The government seeks to justify the ban under s. 1. The court must first assess Stage 1 of the Oakes test.

What does the 'pressing and substantial' requirement at Stage 1 of the Oakes test demand of the government's objective?
opts: Any government objective is pressing and substantial by definition because it was enacted through the democratic process.
The objective must specifically aim to protect national security.

The government's objective must be sufficiently important to warrant overriding a constitutionally protected right; Oakes held the objective must address a real and pressing concern and must not be trivial or merely convenient.
Pressing and substantial simply means the law must have been passed with a legislative majority.
expl: At Stage 1, the Oakes test requires the government to demonstrate its objective is of sufficient importance to override a Charter right. Trivial, minimal, or pretextual objectives fail this threshold. The objective must address a genuine societal concern.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | The Oakes Test — Two-Stage Analysis

text:
A claimant before the Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario argues that a provincial regulation violates s. 15 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. The Tribunal rules on the constitutional question and dismisses the claim. The claimant seeks judicial review, arguing the Tribunal's constitutional analysis was incorrect.

Under the Vavilov framework, what standard of review applies to the Tribunal's determination of a constitutional question?
opts: Reasonableness, because human rights tribunals are specialized bodies and their constitutional analyses should receive the same deference as other decisions.
Correctness, because under Vavilov constitutional questions are one of the narrow categories that attract correctness review regardless of the tribunal's expertise.
Reasonableness, because constitutional questions arising in an administrative proceeding are treated as questions within the tribunal's home statute.
The court has no jurisdiction to review a constitutional finding by an administrative tribunal.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that constitutional questions are one of the narrow categories that rebut the presumption of reasonableness and attract correctness review. On correctness review, the court substitutes its own view for the tribunal's without deference. Constitutional interpretation must be consistent across all adjudicative bodies, which justifies the uniform correctness standard.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65; Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Correctness Standard of Review

text:
A government program provides generous income support to single parents. A person argues that the program implicitly excludes non-binary individuals because its application form does not recognize a non-binary gender category, making it effectively impossible for them to apply. No explicit exclusion appears in the statute.

Can the omission of a group from a government benefits program constitute a violation of s. 15 of the Charter?
opts: No. s. 15 applies only to laws that contain an explicit discriminatory classification.
No. The government is not constitutionally required to create any benefit; if it creates a benefit and someone is excluded, s. 15 cannot apply.

Yes. Where a law creates a benefit but fails to extend it to a group on the basis of an enumerated or analogous ground, the resulting underinclusion can deny the 'equal benefit of the law' protected by s. 15(1).
No. Individuals seeking inclusion in a benefit program must proceed under s. 7, not s. 15.
expl: s. 15(1) guarantees the 'equal benefit of the law.' Underinclusion — where a law extends benefits to some groups but excludes others on the basis of an enumerated or analogous ground — is a recognized form of s. 15 discrimination. The absence of an explicit exclusion is not a defence if the practical effect is discriminatory.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia, [1989] 1 SCR 143; Eldridge v British Columbia (AG), [1997] 3 SCR 624
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 15 — Equality Rights and the Andrews Test

text:
The government administers a program that provides additional employment supports to persons with disabilities. An able-bodied individual argues the program discriminates against him on the basis of disability by providing greater benefits to persons with disabilities. The government argues s. 15(2) of the Charter protects the program from s. 15(1) scrutiny.

Is s. 15(2) a defence under s. 1, or does it operate at the s. 15 stage?
opts: s. 15(2) is a defence under s. 1 that the government must prove after a s. 15 violation is established.
s. 15(2) prevents any judicial review of ameliorative programs; courts have no jurisdiction to scrutinize them.

Under s. 15(2), laws or programs designed to ameliorate the conditions of disadvantaged individuals or groups are not discriminatory under s. 15(1); the government does not need to invoke s. 1, but courts will examine whether the program genuinely ameliorates disadvantage.
s. 15(2) applies only to programs that exclusively benefit persons with physical disabilities.
expl: s. 15(2) operates at the s. 15 stage, not under s. 1. If a program genuinely ameliorates the conditions of a disadvantaged group, it is not a s. 15(1) violation at all. Courts retain the ability to review whether the program is genuinely ameliorative and targets an actually disadvantaged group.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia, [1989] 1 SCR 143; R v Kapp, 2008 SCC 41; Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 15 — Equality Rights and the Andrews Test

text:
The government passes a law requiring all licensed gun retailers to record ammunition sales and retain the records for five years. The stated objective is reducing gun violence. A gun store owner challenges the law under the Charter. The government establishes a pressing and substantial objective. At Stage 2, the owner argues there is no rational connection because criminals will ignore the record-keeping requirement.

Does the rational connection branch of the Oakes proportionality test require the government to prove its measure will perfectly achieve the legislative objective?
opts: Yes. If criminals will not comply with the record-keeping requirement, the law cannot be rationally connected to reducing gun crime.

No. The rational connection test asks only whether there is a logical link between the objective and the means chosen; a measure may be imperfect or partially effective and still be rationally connected.
Yes. The government must produce empirical evidence demonstrating the measure will measurably reduce gun violence.
No. Rational connection is automatically satisfied once a pressing and substantial objective is established.
expl: The rational connection test requires that the means not be arbitrary, unfair, or based on irrational considerations; they must be logically designed to further the objective. Perfect effectiveness is not required. Mandatory record-keeping may deter some sales and aid investigation, maintaining a logical link even if non-compliant criminals exist.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | The Oakes Test — Two-Stage Analysis

text:
An Ontario teacher is dismissed and seeks reinstatement through an arbitration proceeding under the collective agreement. The arbitrator rules on a question of property law — specifically, whether the teacher's contractual entitlement to use a school board vehicle constitutes a property interest under Ontario law. The arbitrator's interpretation of property law differs from what a court would hold. The school board argues correctness review should apply.

Does the arbitrator's determination of a foundational common law property question attract correctness review under Vavilov?
opts: No. All arbitral decisions interpreting any legal question attract reasonableness review under Vavilov.
Yes. Any question involving property law is a constitutional question and therefore attracts correctness review.

Possibly yes. Under Vavilov, questions of law of central importance to the legal system that fall outside the tribunal's area of expertise may attract correctness; if the property law question is truly a foundational legal question outside the arbitrator's expertise, correctness may apply — though this category is narrow and requires the question to be truly of central importance.
No. Questions of contract interpretation, including collective agreements, always attract reasonableness review and property law arising incidentally is subsumed within that analysis.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, one of the narrow categories attracting correctness review is a question of law of central importance to the legal system that falls outside the tribunal's area of expertise. If the arbitrator's interpretation of a foundational property law concept raises a question that transcends the labour relations context and requires consistent application across the legal system, correctness may apply. However, Vavilov cautions this category is narrow; courts must determine whether the question is truly of central importance and genuinely outside the decision-maker's specialized expertise.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Correctness Standard of Review

text:
