A provincial statute provides a monthly child-care subsidy only to single mothers. A single father with a similar income and child-care needs applies for the subsidy and is refused. He challenges the law under s. 15 of the Charter, alleging discrimination on the basis of sex.

Does the single father have a potentially valid s. 15 claim?
opts: No. s. 15 was designed only to protect historically disadvantaged groups, and men are not such a group.
No. Sex-based distinctions that favour women are automatically protected by s. 15(2) as ameliorative programs.

Possibly yes. The law draws a distinction based on sex, an enumerated ground; single fathers denied the benefit may establish a s. 15 violation if the distinction perpetuates prejudice or stereotyping; s. 15(2) may protect the program only if it genuinely ameliorates the conditions of a disadvantaged group.
Yes. Any sex-based distinction automatically violates s. 15.
expl: s. 15 prohibits discrimination on the basis of sex and applies to both men and women. The claimant must still satisfy the two-part test: (1) distinction on sex (an enumerated ground), and (2) the distinction perpetuates prejudice or stereotyping. The government may rely on s. 15(2) if the program is a genuine ameliorative program targeting a disadvantaged group, but s. 15(2) is not a blanket shield for all sex-based distinctions in favour of women.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia, [1989] 1 SCR 143; R v Kapp, 2008 SCC 41; Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 15 — Equality Rights and the Andrews Test

text:
A specialized immigration tribunal has developed an internal procedure for processing a particular class of refugee claims efficiently, using a streamlined paper-based process. An applicant argues that this procedure is insufficient given the importance of the decision (Baker Factor 1 and Factor 3) and requests a full oral hearing.

How does Baker Factor 5 — the decision-maker's own procedural choices — interact with the other Baker factors?
opts: The decision-maker's own procedural choices always prevail over and displace the requirements indicated by the other Baker factors.
Factor 5 has no independent weight; courts apply only Factors 1 through 4 when assessing procedural fairness.

Factor 5 — the decision-maker's choice of procedure — is one of five relevant factors and will be given some deference, particularly where the tribunal has specialized procedural expertise; however, it cannot override the minimum level of fairness required by the other factors combined.
If the decision-maker adopted its procedure in good faith and for legitimate administrative reasons, that automatically satisfies the Baker duty of fairness.
expl: Baker identified five factors, all of which are weighed together. Factor 5 — the decision-maker's own procedural choices — is relevant and courts will give it some deference, particularly for specialized tribunals. However, it is one factor among five and cannot override the minimum procedural fairness required by the overall balancing exercise. A decision-maker cannot contract out of the duty of fairness through an internal policy.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | The Duty of Fairness and Baker Factors

text:
A court finds that a federal law violates s. 7 of the Charter because it is arbitrary — there is no real connection between the law and its stated objective. The government argues that the violation should be saved under s. 1 because the law serves an important societal goal.

Can a violation of s. 7 principles of fundamental justice be justified under s. 1 of the Charter?
opts: Yes. All Charter violations, including s. 7 violations, can be saved under s. 1 through the Oakes test.
No. s. 1 can never apply to s. 7; s. 7 violations are automatically unconstitutional without possibility of justification.

In theory s. 1 applies to all Charter rights including s. 7, but courts have consistently held that a law which violates the principles of fundamental justice — such as being arbitrary, overbroad, or grossly disproportionate — will almost never be saved under s. 1, as such a law cannot constitute a reasonable limit in a free and democratic society.
Yes. The government need only establish a pressing and substantial objective to save a s. 7 violation under s. 1.
expl: While s. 1 technically applies to all Charter rights, courts have consistently recognized that a law violating fundamental justice (i.e., being arbitrary, overbroad, or grossly disproportionate) is virtually impossible to justify under s. 1. A law that is arbitrary, for example, cannot be a 'reasonable limit' in a free and democratic society. This near-impossibility reflects the foundational importance of fundamental justice.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 7 — Life, Liberty, and Security of the Person

text:
Parliament enacts legislation imposing a mandatory minimum custodial sentence of one year for possession of cannabis for the purpose of trafficking. An offender convicted under the provision argues it violates s. 7 of the Charter because the mandatory minimum removes judicial discretion.

What threshold must be established before a court undertakes a s. 7 analysis?
opts: The law must first be found to violate s. 12 of the Charter (cruel and unusual treatment) before s. 7 analysis is triggered.
The government's conduct must deprive the individual of life, liberty, or security of the person; a mandatory term of imprisonment clearly engages the liberty interest under s. 7.
s. 7 is only engaged if the deprivation is caused by direct physical force by a state actor.
s. 7 is only engaged if the mandatory sentence exceeds five years of imprisonment.
expl: Before the principles of fundamental justice are examined, the court must first find that the impugned state action deprives the person of life, liberty, or security of the person. Imprisonment — including a mandatory minimum — clearly constitutes a deprivation of liberty, engaging s. 7.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 7 — Life, Liberty, and Security of the Person

text:
A statutory tribunal that adjudicates environmental permit applications has an applicant arguing that the Baker factors, particularly Factors 1 and 3, mandate the highest level of procedural fairness. The applicant claims this requires a full oral hearing with oral reasons rendered from the bench immediately after the hearing.

Does a finding that Baker factors favour a high level of procedural fairness automatically require a full oral hearing with immediate oral reasons?
opts: Yes. Where Baker factors point to the highest level of fairness, the tribunal must replicate full court-like procedure, including oral reasons delivered immediately.
No. Even where Baker factors support a high level of fairness, the specific procedural requirements remain contextual; written reasons delivered after the hearing may satisfy the duty of fairness.
Yes. Once the highest level of Baker fairness is triggered, the administrative proceeding must mirror a Superior Court trial.
No. Where Baker factors are high, no reasons at all are required because requiring reasons would place an undue burden on administrative decision-makers.
expl: Baker establishes a spectrum of procedural fairness, not a binary choice. Even where the factors point to a higher level of fairness, the specific procedural requirements are determined contextually. Written reasons, provided after deliberation, can satisfy the duty of fairness even in high-stakes cases. The administrative context is distinct from a court proceeding.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | The Duty of Fairness and Baker Factors

text:
A National Parole Board panel denies an offender early release on the basis that he presents an 'undue risk.' The panel's reasons identify two incidents from the offender's institutional file as the primary basis for its conclusion. However, the offender's counsel points out on judicial review that the panel's written reasons contain a significant internal contradiction: one passage states the offender 'has demonstrated meaningful rehabilitative progress' and another states 'the panel finds no evidence of rehabilitation.' The outcome — denial — is the same in both passages.

Is internal incoherence in a tribunal's reasons, standing alone, sufficient to render the decision unreasonable under Vavilov?
opts: No. Internal contradictions in reasons are a minor drafting error and cannot be a basis for quashing a decision that reaches a defensible outcome.
No. Under Vavilov, the court focuses solely on whether the outcome is correct, not on the quality of the reasons.

Yes. Under Vavilov, internal incoherence in a tribunal's reasons — where the reasoning cannot be reconciled — prevents the reviewing court from understanding the analytical path taken by the decision-maker and can render the decision unreasonable as insufficiently justified and intelligible.
Yes. Any inconsistency in administrative reasons automatically voids the decision without further analysis.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the Supreme Court of Canada emphasized that reasonableness review looks at the decision and its reasons together. A decision is unreasonable if its reasons fail to provide a coherent and rational chain of analysis. Where a tribunal's reasons contain fundamental internal contradictions — such as simultaneously finding both that rehabilitation was demonstrated and that there was no evidence of rehabilitation — the reviewing court cannot follow the reasoning process, and the decision may be unreasonable on grounds of internal incoherence.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Reasonableness Standard of Review

text:
A provincial law prohibiting certain political advertising during election periods is challenged under the Charter. The government argues the law is a valid limit on freedom of expression.

Who bears the burden of justifying a limit on a Charter right under s. 1 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms?
opts: The individual claimant must prove the law is unjustified.
The burden is shared equally between the government and the claimant.
The government bears the burden of demonstrating, on a balance of probabilities, that the limitation on the Charter right is reasonable and justified in a free and democratic society.
No burden of proof applies under s. 1; the court assesses the law objectively.
expl: Under s. 1 of the Charter and as confirmed in R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103, the government bears the onus of proving, on a balance of probabilities, that a Charter infringement is a reasonable limit that is demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | The Oakes Test — Two-Stage Analysis

text:
An Ontario social assistance review tribunal holds a hearing and issues a decision denying benefits. Its written reasons state only: 'Having considered the evidence, the panel finds the appellant is not eligible for benefits under the Act.' The applicant seeks judicial review, arguing the reasons are inadequate. The tribunal argues the decision is legally correct and the outcome is defensible even if the reasoning is sparse.

Are reasons that merely state a conclusion, without any analysis of the evidence or applicable legal test, adequate to satisfy the duty of fairness?
opts: Yes. A tribunal satisfies the duty of fairness by providing any written reasons, regardless of their content or analytical depth.
Yes. As long as the outcome of the decision is correct and defensible on the record, inadequate reasons do not constitute a breach of procedural fairness.

No. Adequate reasons must be intelligible, transparent, and justified; reasons that merely state a conclusion without any analysis of the evidence or applicable legal standard fail to satisfy the duty of fairness and may render the decision reviewable as procedurally inadequate.
No. Any deficiency in reasons automatically voids a tribunal decision without consideration of the record or the outcome.
expl: Under Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817, and as reinforced by the adequacy of reasons standard adopted in Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, reasons must be intelligible, transparent, and justified. Reasons that recite only a bare conclusion, without explaining what evidence was weighed or how the legal test was applied, are inadequate. Such reasons frustrate the losing party's ability to understand the basis for the decision and to exercise rights of review.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Administrative Law — Principles and Natural Justice | Right to Reasons for Administrative Decisions

text:
The Ontario College of Teachers holds a disciplinary hearing and suspends a teacher for six months for professional misconduct. The teacher seeks judicial review of the suspension, arguing the tribunal's decision was wrong. The enabling statute does not contain a statutory right of appeal. No constitutional question is raised.

Under the Vavilov framework, what is the presumptive standard of review that applies to the Ontario College of Teachers' disciplinary decision?
opts: Correctness, because professional discipline decisions involve questions of law that courts are best positioned to answer.
Reasonableness, because under Vavilov the default standard for all administrative decisions is reasonableness, absent a specific reason to rebut the presumption.
Patent unreasonableness, which was the standard applied under the pre-Vavilov framework and continues to apply in Ontario.
Correctness, because professional regulatory bodies always attract correctness review as they adjudicate questions that affect the public interest.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the Supreme Court of Canada established that reasonableness is the presumptive default standard of review for all administrative decisions. The presumption is rebutted only in narrow categories: a statutory right of appeal on a question of law, a constitutional question, a question of central importance to the legal system outside the tribunal's expertise, or a jurisdictional question between tribunals. None of those categories apply here.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Reasonableness Standard of Review

text:
A provincial public health regulation requires individuals to submit to a mandatory medical examination conducted by a public health officer, without their consent and without prior judicial authorization, under penalty of a fine. An individual refuses and challenges the regulation under s. 7 of the Charter.

Does the mandatory medical examination engage 'security of the person' under s. 7 of the Charter?
opts: No. Security of the person under s. 7 protects only against criminal punishment such as imprisonment.

Yes. Security of the person includes the right to control what happens to one's body; a state-compelled medical examination without consent constitutes a deprivation of bodily integrity that engages s. 7.
No. Security of the person is only relevant where the penalty for non-compliance is imprisonment.
Only if the examination involves an invasive surgical procedure.
expl: Security of the person under s. 7 encompasses bodily integrity and the right to make decisions about one's own body. As established in R v Morgentaler, [1988] 1 SCR 30, state interference with bodily integrity and serious psychological effects of state action can constitute a deprivation of security of the person. Mandatory medical examination without consent engages s. 7.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 7 — Life, Liberty, and Security of the Person

text:
A municipal by-law prohibits all chalking of messages on public sidewalks. A political advocacy group uses washable chalk to write messages protesting a development project on a city sidewalk. The city issues a fine. The group argues the by-law infringes their freedom of expression under s. 2(b) of the Charter. The city argues the sidewalk is not a protected expressive forum.

Does the use of washable chalk on a public sidewalk to convey a political message attract the protection of s. 2(b) of the Charter?
opts: No. s. 2(b) protects only verbal and written speech; expressive acts such as chalking on public property are excluded.
No. Public sidewalks are government property and are therefore not forums where expressive rights apply.

Yes. Under s. 2(b) of the Charter, any activity that conveys or attempts to convey meaning is expression; political chalking on a public sidewalk is a form of expression whose content and form are both protected, unless the expression takes the form of violence.
Yes, but only if the message relates to a recognized political party or electoral matter.
expl: Under s. 2(b) of the Charter and as affirmed in Irwin Toy Ltd v Quebec (AG), [1989] 1 SCR 927, freedom of expression protects any activity that conveys or attempts to convey meaning, in both its content and its form. The only category excluded from s. 2(b) protection is expression that takes the form of violence. Washable chalk on a public sidewalk is a peaceful form of expressive activity clearly within s. 2(b).
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 2(b); Irwin Toy Ltd v Quebec (AG), [1989] 1 SCR 927
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights | s. 2 — Fundamental Freedoms

text:
A provincial statute requires all individuals who wish to practice as real estate agents to hold a provincial licence. An unlicensed individual is fined and barred from practice. The individual argues the licensing requirement violates their liberty under s. 7 of the Charter because it prevents them from earning a living.

Does a professional licensing requirement engage the 'liberty' interest under s. 7 of the Charter?
opts: Yes. Any restriction on a person's ability to earn a livelihood engages liberty under s. 7.
Yes. Liberty under s. 7 includes the freedom to pursue any lawful occupation without government restriction.

Generally no. Liberty under s. 7 is not engaged by economic rights such as the ability to practice a profession; s. 7 is concerned with physical freedom and personal autonomy in fundamental life choices, not purely economic restrictions.
Yes, but only if the penalty for practicing without a licence includes a term of imprisonment.
expl: Canadian courts have consistently held that s. 7 liberty does not protect purely economic interests such as the right to earn a living or practice a profession. Liberty under s. 7 is concerned with freedom from physical restraint and personal autonomy in fundamental life decisions, not economic freedom. The right to practice real estate without a licence is an economic right that does not engage s. 7.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 7
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 7 — Life, Liberty, and Security of the Person

text:
A provincial labour relations statute prohibits essential-services workers from engaging in collective bargaining over wages, limiting negotiation to working conditions. A union representing paramedics argues the statute violates s. 2(d) of the Charter by stripping away a core component of the right to freedom of association. The government argues s. 2(d) protects only the ability to form and join associations, not what the association does.

Does s. 2(d) of the Charter protect the right of workers to engage in a process of collective bargaining?
opts: No. s. 2(d) protects only the right to form and belong to an association; it does not extend to the activities or goals of the association.
No. Labour relations are a matter of provincial jurisdiction; the Charter does not apply to provincial labour statutes.

Yes. Under Health Services and Support — Facilities Subsector Bargaining Assn v British Columbia, 2007 SCC 27, s. 2(d) protects a process of collective bargaining that enables employees to pursue workplace goals; a law that substantially interferes with that process violates s. 2(d).
Yes, but only if the statute completely eliminates collective bargaining rather than limiting it to certain topics.
expl: Under s. 2(d) of the Charter and Health Services and Support — Facilities Subsector Bargaining Assn v British Columbia, 2007 SCC 27, the Supreme Court of Canada held that s. 2(d) protects the right of employees to associate for the purpose of collective bargaining and to engage in a process of good-faith collective bargaining. A law that substantially interferes with that process — such as prohibiting bargaining over wages — constitutes a prima facie violation of s. 2(d), subject to possible justification under s. 1.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 2(d); Health Services and Support — Facilities Subsector Bargaining Assn v British Columbia, 2007 SCC 27
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Charter of Rights and Freedoms — Section 1 and Key Rights | s. 2 — Fundamental Freedoms

text:
A government benefit program provides enhanced monthly payments to individuals under age 65 and a reduced amount to those 65 and over. A 66-year-old man receives the lower benefit and challenges the program under s. 15 of the Charter, arguing age-based discrimination.

What two elements must the man establish to make out a prima facie s. 15 violation?
opts: He must show the law is unfair and that he is treated worse than any other individual under the program.
He must prove the legislature enacted the program with the intent to discriminate against seniors.

He must establish: (1) the law draws a distinction based on an enumerated or analogous ground (here, age under s. 15(1)); and (2) the distinction creates a disadvantage by perpetuating prejudice or stereotyping against him.
He need only show that he receives a lesser benefit than younger recipients, without more.
expl: The modern s. 15 test from R v Kapp, 2008 SCC 41, and Withler v Canada, 2011 SCC 12, requires: (1) the law creates a distinction based on an enumerated or analogous ground; and (2) the distinction is discriminatory in that it perpetuates prejudice or stereotyping. Age is an enumerated ground under s. 15(1).
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 15; Andrews v Law Society of British Columbia, [1989] 1 SCR 143; R v Kapp, 2008 SCC 41; Withler v Canada (Attorney General), 2011 SCC 12
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | s. 15 — Equality Rights and the Andrews Test

text:
An administrative tribunal is considering revoking a dentist's professional licence following a complaint of professional misconduct. Revocation will permanently end the dentist's ability to practice, eliminating his livelihood.

According to the Baker framework, which factor most strongly suggests that a high level of procedural fairness is owed to the dentist?
opts: Factor 1 — the decision is of a legislative nature, suggesting minimal procedural requirements.
Factor 3 — the greater the impact of the decision on the individual's rights, interests, or livelihood, the higher the level of procedural fairness required.
Factor 5 — the tribunal has historically granted broad procedural rights in similar hearings.
Factor 2 — the enabling statute is detailed and technical, indicating Parliament intended robust procedure.
expl: Baker v Canada, [1999] 2 SCR 817, identified the importance of the decision to the individual (Factor 3) as a key determinant of the level of procedural fairness required. Licence revocation that ends a person's livelihood has profound personal consequences, strongly supporting higher procedural protection.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | The Duty of Fairness and Baker Factors

text:
A federal immigration officer makes a final decision on a humanitarian and compassionate application for permanent residence through a review of the written file alone. The applicant is not given an opportunity to respond to concerns identified by the officer. The applicant seeks judicial review, arguing the procedure was inadequate.

How does the Baker framework determine whether the procedure was adequate in this immigration context?
opts: Administrative decisions are never required to include an oral hearing; file review is always procedurally sufficient.
Citizenship and immigration decisions always require a full oral hearing to satisfy procedural fairness.

The five Baker factors determine the required level of fairness; given the high importance of the decision to the individual and the nature of the process, the SCC in Baker held that at minimum the applicant required an opportunity to present relevant information, though not necessarily through an oral hearing.
Baker does not apply to federal immigration decisions; procedural fairness in that context is governed exclusively by statute.
expl: In Baker itself, the Supreme Court applied the five-factor framework and held that the process was inadequate. The Court required at minimum an opportunity to make written submissions and for an impartial decision-maker to consider them. A full oral hearing was not required, but some opportunity to participate was essential given the importance of the decision to Ms. Baker.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 3
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 3 — Administrative Law | The Duty of Fairness and Baker Factors

text:
A federal administrative decision-maker denies an application for a grant without holding a hearing. The applicant requests reasons. The decision-maker argues that because no hearing was held, there is no statutory or common law duty to provide reasons. The applicant argues that the Baker framework nevertheless imposes a duty to give reasons given the significant financial impact of the denial.

Under Baker v Canada, when does the common law duty of fairness require reasons even where no hearing was held?
opts: Baker imposes a general duty to give reasons for all administrative decisions regardless of the process used or the impact on the individual.
Baker held that reasons are never required where no hearing was held, as the hearing is a precondition for any duty to give reasons.

Under Baker, the duty to give reasons is determined contextually by weighing all five Baker factors; where the decision has significant impact on the individual and the process and stakes call for accountability, a common law duty to give reasons may exist even without a formal hearing.
Baker creates a duty to give reasons only where the enabling statute is silent on procedural requirements.
expl: Under Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817, the Supreme Court of Canada recognized that the duty of fairness may include a requirement to give reasons, and that this requirement is determined by weighing the five Baker factors contextually. The existence of an oral hearing is one relevant factor (and its absence weighs against requiring reasons), but significant individual impact and the nature of the decision can still give rise to a duty to provide reasons even where no formal hearing occurred.
caseRef: Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817; Statutory Powers Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. S.22, s. 17
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Administrative Law — Principles and Natural Justice | Right to Reasons for Administrative Decisions

text:
The Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario finds that an employer discriminated against an employee on the basis of disability. The employer seeks judicial review, arguing that the Tribunal 'got it wrong' because, in the employer's view, the evidence clearly did not support a finding of discrimination. The employer argues that because the Tribunal reached the wrong answer, the standard of review should be correctness.

Is the employer correct that a reviewing court should apply the correctness standard whenever it concludes that the tribunal 'got it wrong'?
opts: Yes. If a court reviewing the record believes the tribunal made a clear error in its findings, correctness review is warranted to prevent injustice.
Yes. Human rights decisions involve questions of law of central importance, so correctness always applies to HRTO decisions.

No. Under Vavilov, the standard of review is determined at the outset of the analysis by examining the legislative framework and context, not by the court's view of whether the tribunal reached the right answer; a court that disagrees with the tribunal's factual or legal conclusions applies reasonableness, not correctness.
No, but if the court finds the decision unreasonable, it may substitute its own finding of no discrimination rather than remitting the matter to the tribunal.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the standard of review is determined at the outset by examining the legislative framework — not by whether the court thinks the tribunal made an error. A court's belief that the tribunal 'got it wrong' does not trigger correctness review. The court applies reasonableness, and within that analysis, asks whether the tribunal's decision was justified, transparent, and intelligible and falls within a range of acceptable outcomes. If the court disagrees with the outcome but the decision is otherwise defensible and coherent, the decision should be upheld on reasonableness review.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Reasonableness Standard of Review

text:
A law prohibits the publication of any information that could identify a crime victim, with no exceptions. A news organization challenges the law as an unjustified limit on freedom of expression. The government concedes the law has a pressing and substantial objective and that the measure is rationally connected to that objective. The remaining dispute is whether the law is the least restrictive option.

Which branch of the Oakes proportionality analysis is most directly engaged by the argument that the blanket prohibition with no exceptions is excessive?
opts: Rational connection — whether publication of victims' names is logically connected to victim protection.
Pressing and substantial — whether protecting crime victims is an important enough objective.

Minimal impairment — the government must show the law impairs freedom of expression as little as reasonably possible; a blanket prohibition with no exceptions may fail because a more tailored approach could achieve the same objective with less constitutional cost.
Proportionality of effects — whether the law's benefits to victims outweigh its effects on the news organization.
expl: Minimal impairment (Stage 2(b)) asks whether the government could have achieved its objective through a less restrictive means that was reasonably available. A blanket prohibition with no exceptions is the classic scenario for minimal impairment analysis: if a more tailored rule (e.g., discretionary exceptions for public interest) could achieve the same protection, the law may fail this branch.
caseRef: Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, s. 1; R v Oakes, [1986] 1 SCR 103
lso: LSO Barrister 2026 Study Materials | Chapter 2 — Charter Rights | The Oakes Test — Two-Stage Analysis

text:
The Immigration and Refugee Board denies a refugee claimant's application on the basis that the claimant lacks credibility. The written reasons state that 'the panel does not find the claimant credible' but do not identify which portions of the claimant's testimony were disbelieved or explain what inconsistencies or implausibilities led to that finding. The claimant seeks judicial review.

How does a reviewing court evaluate whether such a credibility finding survives reasonableness review under Vavilov?
opts: The court will uphold the finding because credibility is a factual matter and courts never interfere with factual findings on judicial review.
The court will substitute its own credibility assessment after reading the full hearing transcript.

Under Vavilov, the court assesses whether the decision is justified, transparent, and intelligible; reasons that simply assert a conclusion of non-credibility without identifying the basis for disbelief may be unreasonable because they do not allow the party or the court to understand the decision-maker's reasoning process.
The court will apply the correctness standard because credibility findings in refugee hearings involve questions of law of central importance to the legal system.
expl: Under Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, reasonableness review requires the court to examine whether the decision — including its reasons — is justified, transparent, and intelligible. On reasonableness review, the court examines the reasons actually given, not what the decision-maker could have said. Reasons that merely assert non-credibility without identifying the evidentiary basis do not allow the losing party or the reviewing court to understand the analytical path taken, and may be unreasonable on that basis.
caseRef: Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. J.1, s. 2; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
lso: 2026 LSO Barrister Examination Materials | Judicial Review | Reasonableness Standard of Review
